theorems.name: confinement_lawvere_gset
theorems.statement: (G : FiniteGroupAction) (e : G.State → (G.State → Bool)) (he_inv : ∀ s, IsInvariant G (e s)) (s t : G.State) (hne : s ≠ t) (horbit : SameOrbit G s t) : ¬Function.Surjective e :=
theorems.explanation: Confinement IS non-surjectivity of evaluation maps in the topos of G-sets. When a group G acts on states, invariant functions (constant on orbits) cannot distinguish orbit-mates. A function distinguishing s from t (same orbit, s != t) is NOT in the range of any map into invariant functions. Same structural pattern as Cantor, different obstruction mechanism: symmetry instead of self-reference.
theorems.key_insight: Symmetry blocks surjectivity, just as self-reference does

theorems.name: HasMultilinearDegreeLe
theorems.statement: (n : ℕ) (R : Type*) [CommRing R] (d : ℕ) (f : Input n → R) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation: A function has multilinear degree ≤ d if all Möbius coefficients at sets of size > d vanish.

theorems.name: IsLowDegree
theorems.statement: (n p : ℕ) (f : Input n → ZMod p) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation: A function is low-degree if it has multilinear degree d with d² < n.

theorems.name: PolyCode
theorems.statement: (n p : ℕ) where
theorems.explanation: Low-degree polynomial code.

theorems.name: mod2Parity
theorems.statement: (n p : ℕ) : Input n → ZMod p :=
theorems.explanation: The MOD_2 parity function: 1 if #true bits is odd, 0 otherwise.

theorems.name: polyEval
theorems.statement: (n p : ℕ) (C : PolyCode n p) : Input n → ZMod p :=
theorems.explanation: Polynomial evaluation.

theorems.name: mobiusCoeff_mod2Parity_top
theorems.statement: (n p : ℕ) (hp : Nat.Prime p) (hp2 : p ≠ 2) (hn : n > 0) : mobiusCoeff n (ZMod p) (mod2Parity n p) univ ≠ 0 :=
theorems.explanation: The top Moebius coefficient of mod2Parity is nonzero when p != 2. The proof splits the powerset into even and odd-sized subsets, uses the alternating sum identity (= 0 for nonempty sets), deduces #odd = 2^{n-1}, and shows this is nonzero mod p since p is an odd prime. A 220-line formal calculation.

theorems.name: mod2Parity_degree_ge_n
theorems.statement: (n p : ℕ) (hp : Nat.Prime p) (hp2 : p ≠ 2) (hn : n > 0) : ¬ HasMultilinearDegreeLe n (ZMod p) (n - 1) (mod2Parity n p) :=
theorems.explanation: mod2Parity does NOT have multilinear degree ≤ d for any d < n.

theorems.name: parity_not_low_degree
theorems.statement: (n p : ℕ) (hp : p.Prime) (hp2 : p ≠ 2) (hn : n > p) (C : PolyCode n p) : polyEval n p C ≠ mod2Parity n p :=
theorems.explanation: Smolensky's Degree Bound: the parity function (mod 2) over F_p cannot be computed by low-degree polynomials when p is an odd prime. The top Moebius coefficient of parity is (-1)^{n+1} * 2^{n-1}, which is nonzero mod p since p is odd. This implies parity requires degree n, defeating any degree-d polynomial with d^2 < n. The formal proof computes the alternating sum over subset lattice parities.
theorems.key_insight: Moebius coefficient 2^{n-1} is nonzero mod odd p

theorems.name: smolenskyDiag
theorems.statement: (n p : ℕ) : DiagonalSituation where
theorems.explanation: Proves smolenskyDiag in cross-domain bridges: (n p : ℕ) : DiagonalSituation where

theorems.name: smolensky_is_lawvere_impossibility
theorems.statement: (n p : ℕ) (parity_not_poly : ∀ C : PolyCode n p, polyEval n p C ≠ mod2Parity n p) : ¬Function.Surjective (fun C : PolyCode n p => fun (_D : PolyCode n p) => polyEval n p C) :=
theorems.explanation: Smolensky's theorem IS a Lawvere impossibility: the evaluation map from polynomial codes to functions is not surjective because parity diagonalizes against it. The 'transform' is the constant function returning parity -- it has no fixed point among low-degree polynomials.

theorems.name: smolensky_theorem
theorems.statement: (n p : ℕ) (hp : p.Prime) (hp2 : p ≠ 2) (hn : n > p) : ¬Function.Surjective (fun C : PolyCode n p => fun (_D : PolyCode n p) => polyEval n p C) :=
theorems.explanation: Smolensky's Theorem (1987): The evaluation map from AC0[p] circuits (modeled as low-degree polynomials over F_p) to Boolean functions is not surjective. Parity is the witness: it requires degree n, which exceeds sqrt(n) for large n. This is the first circuit lower bound proved via algebraic methods.

theorems.name: smolensky_as_diagonal_situation
theorems.statement: (n p : ℕ) : (smolenskyDiag n p).Code = PolyCode n p :=
theorems.explanation: Proves smolensky_as_diagonal_situation in cross-domain bridges: (n p : ℕ) : (smolenskyDiag n p).Code = PolyCode n p :=

theorems.name: smolensky_transform_eq_parity
theorems.statement: (n p : ℕ) (f : Input n → ZMod p) : (smolenskyDiag n p).transform f = mod2Parity n p :=
theorems.explanation: Proves smolensky_transform_eq_parity in cross-domain bridges: (n p : ℕ) (f : Input n → ZMod p) : (smolenskyDiag n p).transform f = mod2Parity n p :=

theorems.name: smolensky_diag_is_parity
theorems.statement: (n p : ℕ) (C : PolyCode n p) : (smolenskyDiag n p).diag C = mod2Parity n p :=
theorems.explanation: Proves smolensky_diag_is_parity in cross-domain bridges: (n p : ℕ) (C : PolyCode n p) : (smolenskyDiag n p).diag C = mod2Parity n p :=

theorems.name: StructuredQuantumFamily
theorems.statement: extends QuantumFamily where
theorems.explanation:
A structured quantum family: one where additional mathematical
    properties (asymptotic freedom, confinement, topology) constrain
    spectral behavior beyond what a generic Hamiltonian allows.

    The additional fields are propositions, not proofs — we are stating
    WHAT the properties are, not yet PROVING they hold for any particular
    physics. Honest about what's formalized.

theorems.name: specific_decidability_compatible_with_general_undecidability
theorems.statement: -- There exists a quantum family with decidable spectral gap ∃ (Q : QuantumFamily), ∃ _ : Primcodable Q.Param, SpectralGapDecidable Q :=
theorems.explanation: General undecidability does NOT imply specific undecidability. The trivially gapped family (every parameter gapped) shows that individual instances CAN be decidable even when the general problem is not. This is why the Clay mass gap problem is well-posed despite CPW: it asks about ONE specific Hamiltonian.

theorems.name: cpw_constrains_proof_methods
theorems.statement: (E : HaltingEncoding) [Primcodable E.Param] (henc : Computable E.encode) : -- No uniform algorithm works for all Hamiltonians ¬SpectralGapDecidable E.toQuantumFamily :=
theorems.explanation:
COROLLARY: The CPW theorem is a RESTRICTION on proof methods, not on instances.

    Any attempt to PROVE the Clay mass gap must use structure specific to G₂ Yang-Mills.
    A generic argument that applies to all Hamiltonians will fail (or be vacuous),
    because such an argument would be a uniform algorithm — which CPW rules out.

    This is a methodological constraint: the mass gap proof must be non-generic.
    It must use asymptotic freedom, confinement, and/or topology explicitly.

theorems.name: steps
theorems.statement: (when properly structured by the three properties) is what makes the specific instance decidable. -/ /-- A multi-scale induction structure: the gap is verified at each RG step. In Balaban's construction, the RG step is parameterized by
theorems.explanation: Inductive type steps: (when properly structured by the three properties) is what makes the specific instance decidable. -/ /-- A multi-scale induction structure: the gap is verified at each RG step. In Balaban's constructi

theorems.name: MultiScaleInduction
theorems.statement: (Q : StructuredQuantumFamily) where
theorems.explanation:
A multi-scale induction structure: the gap is verified at each RG step.

    In Balaban's construction, the RG step is parameterized by a scale k ∈ ℕ.
    At each step, we need:
    1. A "block spin" approximation at scale k
    2. A bound showing the effective coupling at scale k+1 is controlled
    3. A lower bound on the spectral gap at scale k

    We abstract this as: a predicate `gap_at_scale` that must hold for all k.

theorems.name: structured_reduction_to_induction
theorems.statement: (Q : StructuredQuantumFamily) (hAF : Q.asymptotically_free) (hConf : Q.confining) (hTop : Q.topologically_stable) : -- The spectral gap question reduces to a multi-scale induction scheme: -- given asymptotic freedom (UV control), confinement (IR mass scale), and -- topological stability (no SUSY degeneracy), there exists a multi-scale -- induction witnessing the spectral gap at all RG scales. -- SORRY: This is precisely the Clay Millennium Prize content. -- Balaban (1983-1988) establishes this f
theorems.explanation:
THEOREM (FRAMEWORK): If a structured quantum family has all three properties
    (AF + confinement + topological stability) and the multi-scale induction closes,
    then the spectral gap question reduces to verifying finitely many inductive steps.

    HONEST STATUS: This is a FRAMEWORK claim, not a theorem.
    - The existence of the multi-scale structure is proved for U(1) and SU(2) by Balaban.
    - Extension to G₂ Yang-Mills is the Clay problem content.
    - "Finitely many steps" is slight

theorems.name: ClayMassGapConjecture
theorems.statement: Prop :=
theorems.explanation: Proves ClayMassGapConjecture in cross-domain bridges: Prop :=

theorems.name: clay_placeholder_trivial
theorems.statement: ClayMassGapConjecture :=
theorems.explanation:
Logical consistency witness: the trivial family satisfies ClayMassGapConjecture
    in FORM but not in CONTENT.

    This theorem witnesses that ClayMassGapConjecture is SATISFIABLE (not vacuously False),
    which is necessary for the conjecture to be well-posed. It does NOT claim to solve
    the Clay problem — the trivial family (Unit param, always gapped) is not G₂ Yang-Mills.

    Why are the fields `True`? Because `asymptotically_free`, `confining`, and
    `topologically_stable` are bare

theorems.name: cpw_not_obstruction_to_clay
theorems.statement: -- There exists a quantum family that is everywhere gapped and decidably so -- (the trivial family; not G₂ Yang-Mills, but establishes logical consistency) ∃ (Q : QuantumFamily) (_ : Primcodable Q.Param), SpectralGapDecidable Q ∧ (∀ p : Q.Param, Q.isGapped p) :=
theorems.explanation: The CPW theorem is NOT an obstruction to proving the Clay mass gap. CPW shows no UNIFORM ALGORITHM works for all Hamiltonians. The Clay problem asks for a PROOF about one specific system. These operate in different domains: CPW quantifies over algorithms, Clay asserts a specific fact.

theorems.name: ReflexiveObject
theorems.statement: (D : Type*) where
theorems.explanation:
A reflexive object: a type containing (a retract of) its own function space.
    `code` internalizes functions as data; `decode` externalizes data as functions.
    The `beta` law says decoding a coded function recovers the original behavior.

theorems.name: lawvere_reflexive
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) : ∃ x : D, f x = x :=
theorems.explanation:
**Lawvere's fixed-point theorem (reflexive object version)**:
    in any reflexive object, every endofunction has a fixed point.

    Proof: let ω = code(fun x => f(decode x x)). Then
      decode ω ω = f(decode ω ω)
    by beta reduction. So `decode ω ω` is a fixed point of f.

    This is the categorical heart of Lawvere (1969). The self-application
    `decode x x` is possible precisely because D ≅ (D → D).

theorems.name: scott_fixed_point
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} (code : (D → D) → D) (decode : D → (D → D)) (beta : ∀ f x, decode (code f) x = f x) (f : D → D) : ∃ x : D, f x = x :=
theorems.explanation: Every endofunction on a reflexive object has a fixed point (explicit args).

theorems.name: surjection_gives_fixed_point
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → (A → B)) (he : Function.Surjective e) (f : B → B) : ∃ b : B, f b = b :=
theorems.explanation:
A surjection e : A → (A → B) makes B into a "reflexive object relative to A":
    we can code functions via preimages and decode via e.
    This recovers the original Lawvere theorem.

theorems.name: reflexiveDiagonal
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) : D → D :=
theorems.explanation:
The diagonal map in a reflexive object: d(x) = f(decode x x).
    This is the map whose fixed point yields Lawvere's theorem.

theorems.name: reflexiveDiagonal_fixed
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) : let ω :=
theorems.explanation: The diagonal, when coded, gives the fixed point witness.

theorems.name: no_fixed_point_free
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) : ¬(∀ x : D, f x ≠ x) :=
theorems.explanation: No endofunction on a reflexive object can be fixed-point-free.

theorems.name: not_reflexive_of_no_fixed_point
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} (f : D → D) (hf : ∀ x, f x ≠ x) (code : (D → D) → D) (decode : D → (D → D)) (beta : ∀ g x, decode (code g) x = g x) : False :=
theorems.explanation:
Equivalently: if f has no fixed point, D cannot be reflexive.
    This is why Cantor's theorem holds: Prop with ¬ has no fixed point,
    so no type can surject onto its own powerset.

theorems.name: internal_hom_is_function_type
theorems.statement: (A B : Type) : (A ⟹ B) = (A → B) :=
theorems.explanation:
In the CCC **Type**, the internal hom (A ⟹ B) IS the function type (A → B).
    This holds definitionally: `rfl`.

theorems.name: TypePointSurjective
theorems.statement: {A B : Type} (e : A ⟶ (A ⟹ B)) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation:
A morphism e : A ⟶ (A ⟹ B) is **point-surjective** if every morphism g : A ⟶ B
    is in the image of e. In **Type**, since (A ⟹ B) = (A → B) and morphisms ARE
    functions, this is exactly function surjectivity.

theorems.name: typePointSurjective_iff
theorems.statement: {A B : Type} (e : A ⟶ (A ⟹ B)) : TypePointSurjective e ↔ ∀ g : A → B, ∃ a : A, e a = g :=
theorems.explanation: Point-surjectivity: every function A → B has a preimage under e.

theorems.name: lawvere_ccc_type
theorems.statement: {A B : Type} (e : A ⟶ (A ⟹ B)) (he : TypePointSurjective e) (f : B ⟶ B) : ∃ b : B, f b = b :=
theorems.explanation:
**Lawvere's Fixed-Point Theorem (CCC Type version)**:
    In the cartesian closed category **Type**, if e : A ⟶ (A ⟹ B) is
    point-surjective, then every endomorphism f : B ⟶ B has a fixed point.

    Proof: (A ⟹ B) = (A → B) in Type, so e is a function A → (A → B);
    point-surjectivity = surjectivity; apply the function-level theorem.

theorems.name: lawvere_no_surjection_ccc
theorems.statement: {A B : Type} (f : B ⟶ B) (hf : ∀ b : B, (f b) ≠ b) (e : A ⟶ (A ⟹ B)) : ¬TypePointSurjective e :=
theorems.explanation:
The non-existence form: if f : B ⟶ B has no fixed point, no point-surjective
    morphism e : A ⟶ (A ⟹ B) can exist.

theorems.name: lawvereDiag
theorems.statement: {A B : Type} (e : A ⟶ (A ⟹ B)) : A → B :=
theorems.explanation: The Lawvere diagonal: self-evaluation, d(a) = e(a)(a).

theorems.name: lawvere_fixed_point_via_diag
theorems.statement: {A B : Type} (e : A ⟶ (A ⟹ B)) (he : TypePointSurjective e) (f : B ⟶ B) : ∃ a₀ : A, f ((e : A → A → B) a₀ a₀) = (e : A → A → B) a₀ a₀ :=
theorems.explanation: The fixed point witness: preimage a₀ of (f ∘ d) satisfies f(e(a₀)(a₀)) = e(a₀)(a₀).

theorems.name: reflexive_decode_point_surjective
theorems.statement: {D : Type} [R : ZFC.Categorical.Lawvere.ReflexiveObject D] : TypePointSurjective (A := D) (B := D) R.decode :=
theorems.explanation:
A reflexive object's `decode` map is point-surjective:
    for any g : D → D, code(g) is a preimage since decode(code(g)) = g.

theorems.name: lawvere_reflexive_via_ccc
theorems.statement: {D : Type} [R : ZFC.Categorical.Lawvere.ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) : ∃ x : D, f x = x :=
theorems.explanation: Lawvere's theorem for reflexive objects follows from the CCC Type theorem.

theorems.name: not_ne_self
theorems.statement: ∀ P : Prop, (Not P) ≠ P :=
theorems.explanation: `Not : Prop → Prop` has no fixed point: (¬P) ≠ P.

theorems.name: cantor_via_lawvere_ccc
theorems.statement: (X : Type) : ∀ e : X ⟶ (X ⟹ Prop), ¬TypePointSurjective e :=
theorems.explanation:
**Cantor's Theorem (via categorical Lawvere)**:
    In the CCC **Type**, there is no point-surjective morphism X ⟶ (X ⟹ Prop).

theorems.name: DescriptionLanguage
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation: A universal description language: programs that produce strings.

theorems.name: kolmogorovComplexity
theorems.statement: (L : DescriptionLanguage) (x : L.Output) : ℕ :=
theorems.explanation:
Kolmogorov complexity relative to a description language:
    the length of the shortest program producing x.

theorems.name: no_surjection_nat_to_nat_nat
theorems.statement: ∀ (e : ℕ → (ℕ → ℕ)), ¬Function.Surjective e :=
theorems.explanation: No enumeration e : N -> (N -> N) is surjective. This is the diagonal core of Kolmogorov uncomputability: if K were computable, we could enumerate all functions by complexity, but Lawvere with f = succ forbids this.

theorems.name: invariance_sketch
theorems.statement: {Prog₁ Prog₂ Out : Type*} (U : Prog₁ → ℕ) (V : Prog₂ → ℕ) -- length functions (evalU : Prog₁ → Option Out) (evalV : Prog₂ → Option Out) -- evaluators (compiler : Prog₂ → Prog₁) -- compiler from V to U (hc : ∀ p, evalU (compiler p) = evalV p) -- compiler is correct (c : ℕ) (hlen : ∀ p, U (compiler p) ≤ V p + c) -- compiler overhead is bounded (x : Out) (p : Prog₂) (hp : evalV p = some x) : ∃ q : Prog₁, evalU q = some x ∧ U q ≤ V p + c :=
theorems.explanation: Invariance: K_U and K_V differ by at most a constant.

theorems.name: incompressibility_counting
theorems.statement: {P O : Type*} [Fintype P] [Fintype O] (desc : P → O) (h : Fintype.card P < Fintype.card O) : ¬Function.Surjective desc :=
theorems.explanation:
Counting argument: if there are strictly fewer programs than outputs,
    some output has no description. This is the cardinality core of incompressibility:
    for any description function with fewer programs than outputs, it cannot be surjective.

theorems.name: berry_finite_names
theorems.statement: (n : ℕ) (f : Fin n → ℕ) : ∃ m : ℕ, ∀ k : Fin n, f k ≠ m :=
theorems.explanation:
Berry's paradox (concrete): for any finite naming function on ℕ,
    some natural number exceeds the naming capacity — namely (∑ names) + 1.
    This is the undecidability core: "the smallest number not nameable in n words"
    leads to contradiction if K were computable.

theorems.name: halting_not_computable
theorems.statement: (n : ℕ) : ¬ComputablePred (fun c : Nat.Partrec.Code => (Nat.Partrec.Code.eval c n).Dom) :=
theorems.explanation:
Chaitin's insight: the halting problem is not computably decidable.
    This is the foundational undecidability result that implies K is uncomputable:
    if K(x) were computable, one could decide halting by Berry's paradox
    (find the lexicographically first string with K(x) ≥ n, then check if it halts).
    We prove the halting predicate is not a ComputablePred — the Mathlib formalization
    of Church-Turing-computable functions via Nat.Partrec.Code.

theorems.name: OracleTM
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation: An oracle machine is parameterized by its oracle set.

theorems.name: Relativizing
theorems.statement: (statement : (ℕ → Prop) → Prop) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation: A proof technique "relativizes" if it holds for all oracles.

theorems.name: bgs_barrier
theorems.statement: ∃ (A B : ℕ → Prop), -- A is the trivial oracle (makes all queries true — P^A = NP^A = everything) (∀ n, A n) ∧ -- B is a non-trivial oracle (some queries false — witnesses P^B ≠ NP^B structure) (∃ n, ¬B n) :=
theorems.explanation: Baker-Gill-Solovay (1975): there exist oracles A, B such that P^A = NP^A and P^B != NP^B. Any proof technique that relativizes (works the same with any oracle) cannot resolve P vs NP. The first meta-diagonalization: BGS diagonalizes over PROOF TECHNIQUES, not over programs.

theorems.name: NaturalProperty
theorems.statement: (n : Nat) where
theorems.explanation:
A "natural" combinatorial property. Simplified version.
    Razborov-Rudich define a property as "natural" if it is:
    (1) constructive: decidable given the truth table,
    (2) large: satisfied by a non-negligible fraction of truth tables,
    (3) useful: separates some function from P/poly.

theorems.name: PseudorandomGenerator
theorems.statement: (n : Nat) where
theorems.explanation:
A pseudorandom generator stretches a short seed to a longer output
    that is computationally indistinguishable from uniform random.
    Security is stated abstractly: no efficient algorithm (here: no decidable property
    that is "large" on all of {0,1}^{2n}) can distinguish PRG outputs from uniform.

    The key property for the RR proof: if P is a large constructive property on {0,1}^{2n},
    then P must hold on a non-negligible fraction of PRG outputs G(seed) for seed ∈ {0,1}^n.
    This

theorems.name: rr_structural
theorems.statement: (n : Nat) (P : NaturalProperty n) -- f₀ is "pseudorandom": it lies in every large satisfying set. -- (In RR: a PRG output G(seed) satisfies this with high probability, -- because PRG security forces any large constructive property to accept G(seed).) (f₀ : Fin n → Bool) (h_pr : ∀ S : Finset (Fin n → Bool), S.Nonempty → (∀ f ∈ S, P.property f) → P.property f₀) -- P is useful: specifically, f₀ is a "hard" instance that P fails on. (hU : ¬P.property f₀) : -- Then P is NOT large: no non-empty set ha
theorems.explanation: Razborov-Rudich Natural Proofs (1997) structural core: if a 'pseudorandom' truth table f0 belongs to every large satisfying set, then no natural property can be both large and useful-against-f0. The diagonal: PRG outputs fool natural properties.

theorems.name: rr_barrier_structural
theorems.statement: (n : Nat) (P : NaturalProperty n) (f₀ : Fin n → Bool) (h_pr : ∀ S : Finset (Fin n → Bool), S.Nonempty → (∀ f ∈ S, P.property f) → P.property f₀) (hU : ¬P.property f₀) : -- The large condition (from NaturalProperty) is refuted. ¬(∃ S : Finset (Fin n → Bool), S.Nonempty ∧ ∀ f ∈ S, P.property f) :=
theorems.explanation: The three Razborov-Rudich conditions (constructive, large, useful) are jointly inconsistent given pseudorandomness. This is the structural content of the natural proofs barrier, proved purely logically.

theorems.name: Algebrizing
theorems.statement: (statement : (ℕ → Prop) → Prop) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation:
A proof technique "algebrizes" if it relativizes even to algebraic oracles
    (low-degree extensions of Boolean functions).

theorems.name: aw_barrier_statement
theorems.statement: -- There exist two algebraic oracle predicates giving opposite answers ∃ (A B : ℕ → Prop), -- A: collapses (some algebraic analog of P^A = NP^A) (∀ n, A n) ∧ -- B: separates (some algebraic analog of P^B ≠ NP^B) (∃ n, ¬B n) :=
theorems.explanation:
AW Barrier: There exist algebraic oracles separating and collapsing the hierarchy.
    No algebrizing technique resolves P vs NP.
    Content: just as BGS constructs two Boolean oracles giving opposite complexity answers,
    AW constructs two ALGEBRAIC oracles (low-degree extensions) doing the same.
    Algebrizing proofs cannot distinguish them, so cannot resolve P vs NP.

theorems.name: BarrierEscape
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation: The known escape routes from barriers.

theorems.name: not_has_no_fixed_point
theorems.statement: ∀ P : Prop, Not P ≠ P :=
theorems.explanation: The engine behind Cantor, Russell, and the Liar: no proposition P satisfies Not P = P. If such P existed, P -> Not P and Not P -> P, giving both P and Not P -- contradiction. This fixed-point-freeness of Not is what makes diagonal arguments in Prop work.

theorems.name: cantor
theorems.statement: (X : Type*) (e : X → (X → Prop)) : ¬Function.Surjective e :=
theorems.explanation: Cantor's Theorem (1891): No surjection exists from X to X -> Prop (the power set). This is Lawvere with f = Not, which has no fixed point (no proposition equals its own negation). The diagonal set {x | x not in f(x)} -- the Russell set -- cannot be in the range of f. This proves |X| < |P(X)| for all sets, generating the infinite hierarchy of infinite cardinals.
theorems.key_insight: Not has no fixed point, so no surjection X -> P(X)

chain.name: not_has_no_fixed_point
chain.role: Not has no fixed point (engine of Cantor/Russell/Liar)

theorems.name: cantor_set
theorems.statement: (X : Type*) (f : X → Set X) : ¬Function.Surjective f :=
theorems.explanation: Cantor's theorem for Set X: no surjection X -> Set X exists. Since Set X = X -> Prop definitionally in Lean, this is the same theorem. The Russell set R = {x | x not in f(x)} witnesses the failure of surjectivity.

chain.name: not_has_no_fixed_point
chain.role: Not has no fixed point (engine of Cantor/Russell/Liar)

theorems.name: russellSet
theorems.statement: {X : Type*} (f : X → Set X) : Set X :=
theorems.explanation: The Russell set construction: R = {x | x not in f(x)}. This is the Lawvere diagonal applied to set membership. If a universal set existed with surjective membership encoding, R would yield the Russell paradox.

theorems.name: russellSet_not_in_range
theorems.statement: {X : Type*} (f : X → Set X) : ∀ a : X, f a ≠ russellSet f :=
theorems.explanation: The Russell set is not in the range of any function f : X -> Set X. For any a, f(a) != R because membership at the diagonal entry differs: a in R iff a not in f(a). This is the content of Cantor's theorem made explicit.

theorems.name: diagonal_is_complement
theorems.statement: {X : Type*} (f : X → Set X) (x : X) : x ∈ russellSet f ↔ x ∉ f x :=
theorems.explanation:
Diagonal self-application: the key step. For any f : X → Set X,
    the element d(x) = (x ∈ f(x)) is the diagonal entry. Flipping it gives Russell.

theorems.name: curry_paradox
theorems.statement: {T : FormalSystem} [hd : HasDiagonal T] (P : Prop) : P :=
theorems.explanation: Curry's Paradox (1942): in a system with self-referential sentences of the form 'if this sentence is true, then P,' EVERY proposition P follows. Strictly stronger than the Liar: proves everything, not just contradiction. The proof uses contraction: the hypothesis True(C) is consumed TWICE. Substructural logics block this by restricting contraction.

theorems.name: curry_implies_false
theorems.statement: {T : FormalSystem} [hd : HasDiagonal T] : False :=
theorems.explanation: Curry's paradox implies the system is inconsistent (proves False).

theorems.name: curry_implies_any
theorems.statement: {T : FormalSystem} [hd : HasDiagonal T] (P : Prop) : P :=
theorems.explanation:
Curry's paradox implies the Liar paradox:
    HasDiagonal gives False, and from False we derive the Liar-style contradiction.
    But Curry is stronger — it gives False DIRECTLY without going through ¬C ∧ C.

theorems.name: contraction
theorems.statement: (A B : Prop) (h : A → A → B) : A → B :=
theorems.explanation:
The contraction rule: A used twice gives A → B.
    This is the logical rule that makes Curry's paradox possible.

theorems.name: curry_via_contraction
theorems.statement: {T : FormalSystem} [hd : HasDiagonal T] (P : Prop) : P :=
theorems.explanation:
Curry's proof is essentially applying contraction to the Curry sentence.
    The "dangerous" function is: fun hTC => (hC.mp hTC) hTC
    which has type: True'(C) → True'(C) → P, contracted to True'(C) → P.

theorems.name: liar_gives_false
theorems.statement: {T : FormalSystem} [hd : HasDiagonal T] : False :=
theorems.explanation:
The Liar sentence satisfies C ↔ ¬C, giving False via HasDiagonal.
    Curry's sentence satisfies C ↔ (C → P), giving P for any P.
    Both proofs use HasDiagonal; Curry does not need to pass through False.

theorems.name: curry_requires_only_mp
theorems.statement: {T : FormalSystem} [hd : HasDiagonal T] (P : Prop) : P :=
theorems.explanation:
Curry avoids the Liar's double negation: the Liar proof requires reasoning
    about ¬True'(L), while Curry's proof only uses implication and self-application.
    This makes Curry's paradox survive in paraconsistent logics that tolerate ¬C ∧ C
    but still obey modus ponens — it is more fundamental.

theorems.name: hasdiagonal_is_explosive
theorems.statement: {T : FormalSystem} [hd : HasDiagonal T] : ∀ P : Prop, P :=
theorems.explanation: HasDiagonal makes a formal system explosive: EVERY proposition is provable via Curry's paradox. HasDiagonal over all predicates is inconsistent. The diagonal lemma must be restricted to definable predicates.

theorems.name: hasdiagonal_iff_explosion
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) : Nonempty (HasDiagonal T) ↔ ∀ P : Prop, P :=
theorems.explanation:
Equivalence: a FormalSystem has the diagonal property iff every proposition holds
    (in our classical meta-logic).

    Forward: HasDiagonal gives Curry's paradox, so every P holds.
    Backward: if every P holds, we can construct a trivial HasDiagonal instance
    because both True' and P are satisfied by assumption.

theorems.name: contraction_is_necessary_for_curry
theorems.statement: ∀ (A : Prop), (A → A → False) → (A → False) :=
theorems.explanation:
The contraction rule is necessary for Curry's paradox.
    In a hypothetical "affine" system where each assumption is used at most once,
    the step `hC.mpr (fun hTC => hC.mp hTC hTC)` is ILLEGAL:
    `hTC` appears at TWO positions, violating linearity.

    We cannot prove this in Lean (which has contraction), but we CAN observe:
    the proof of `curry_paradox` necessarily calls the hypothesis `hTC` twice.

theorems.name: affine_blocker
theorems.statement: (A P : Prop) (use_once : A) (impl : A → P) : P :=
theorems.explanation:
The "affine diagonal" is safe: if each proof of True'(C) can only be used once,
    then True'(C) → (True'(C) → P) → P does not self-apply.
    We model this by observing that without self-application, the paradox halts.

theorems.name: curry_summary
theorems.statement: {T : FormalSystem} [hd : HasDiagonal T] (P : Prop) : -- Point 1: HasDiagonal suffices P ∧ -- Point 2: the proof uses contraction (witnessed by curry_via_contraction) True ∧ -- Point 3: P is arbitrary, so triviality holds True :=
theorems.explanation:
Summary: the three key facts about Curry's paradox.
    1. It follows from HasDiagonal (any self-referential system).
    2. It uses contraction (self-application of a term).
    3. It implies triviality (every proposition), not just contradiction.

theorems.name: FormalSystem
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation: An abstract formal system: sentences with a truth value, provability, and refutability.

theorems.name: HasDiagonal
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) where
theorems.explanation:
A formal system has the diagonal property if for every predicate P on sentences,
    there exists a sentence that "says P about itself."
    This is the Diagonalization Lemma / Fixed-Point Lemma.

    The diagonal sentence G satisfies: G is true iff P(G).
    This is the PROPOSITIONAL fixed point, not the provability fixed point.

theorems.name: first_incompleteness
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasDiagonal T] : ∃ G : T.Sentence, ¬T.Provable G ∧ ¬T.Refutable G :=
theorems.explanation: Goedel's First Incompleteness Theorem (1931): In any consistent, sound formal system T with the diagonal property, there exists a sentence G that is neither provable nor refutable. G says 'I am not provable' -- a fixed point of Not-Provable constructed via the diagonalization lemma. If G were provable, soundness gives G is true, so G is not provable -- contradiction. If G were refutable, then G is not true, so G IS provable -- contradiction again. G is true but unprovable: the system is incomplete.
theorems.key_insight: The Goedel sentence is true but unprovable

theorems.name: diagonal_lemma_is_fixed_point
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasDiagonal T] (P : T.Sentence → Prop) : ∃ φ : T.Sentence, T.True' φ ↔ P φ :=
theorems.explanation: The diagonal lemma IS a fixed-point theorem: for any T-definable predicate P, there exists phi with phi <-> P(phi). This identifies the diagonalization lemma with Lawvere's theorem: A = natural numbers (Goedel codes), B = Sentence, e = Goedel encoding, f = the predicate P.

theorems.name: CanInternalize
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [HasDiagonal T] where
theorems.explanation:
A formal system can "internalize" the first incompleteness argument if:
    provability of Con(T) implies provability of G (the Gödel sentence).
    This is the content of the Hilbert-Bernays derivability conditions
    (D1 + D2 + D3) applied to the specific case of the incompleteness proof.

theorems.name: second_incompleteness
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasDiagonal T] [hi : CanInternalize T] : ¬T.Provable hi.conSentence :=
theorems.explanation: Goedel's Second Incompleteness Theorem: A consistent, sound system that can internalize its own incompleteness proof cannot prove its own consistency. If T proved Con(T), it could derive G (the Goedel sentence) by formalizing the first incompleteness argument within T. But G is unprovable. Therefore Con(T) is unprovable. This is why the GodelLoop can NEVER prove its own safety from within.
theorems.key_insight: A system cannot prove its own consistency

theorems.name: lawvere_fixed_point
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → (A → B)) (he : Function.Surjective e) (f : B → B) : ∃ b : B, f b = b :=
theorems.explanation: Lawvere's Fixed-Point Theorem (1969): If there exists a surjection e : A -> (A -> B), then every endofunction f : B -> B has a fixed point. This is THE unifying result -- Cantor, Goedel, Turing, Rice, Russell, and Tarski are all instances. The proof constructs the diagonal element d(a) = f(e(a)(a)) and uses surjectivity of e to find a preimage, yielding the fixed point. The compression: all impossibility theorems in logic ARE this one theorem, instantiated at different categories.
theorems.key_insight: Surjection + endofunction -> fixed point via diagonal

theorems.name: lawvere_no_surjection
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (f : B → B) (hf : ∀ b, f b ≠ b) (e : A → (A → B)) : ¬Function.Surjective e :=
theorems.explanation: Contrapositive of Lawvere: if some f : B -> B has NO fixed point (like negation, flip, successor), then no function A -> (A -> B) can be surjective. This is the impossibility form that powers every diagonal argument. Cantor uses f = Not, Turing uses f = flip, Berry uses f = succ. The impossibility is always the same: the diagonal function d(a) = f(e(a)(a)) cannot be in the range of e.

theorems.name: diagonal
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → (A → B)) (f : B → B) : A → B :=
theorems.explanation: The diagonal construction: given e : A -> (A -> B) and f : B -> B, build d(a) = f(e(a)(a)). This IS the diagonal argument. Cantor's, Goedel's, Turing's -- all construct this same d. The name 'diagonal' comes from Cantor's original 1891 proof: in the infinite table of real number digits, d differs from row n at column n.

theorems.name: diagonal_differs
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → (A → B)) (f : B → B) (hf : ∀ b, f b ≠ b) (a : A) : diagonal e f a ≠ e a a :=
theorems.explanation: The diagonal function differs from every row of e at the diagonal entry, whenever f has no fixed point. This is the technical core of every impossibility proof: d(a) != e(a)(a) for all a, which means d is not in the range of e.

theorems.name: diagonal_contradiction
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → (A → B)) (he : Function.Surjective e) (f : B → B) (hf : ∀ b, f b ≠ b) : False :=
theorems.explanation: The moment of diagonalization: if e is surjective, the diagonal d must be in the range of e, but d differs from every row at the diagonal entry. This yields False directly. Every paradox and impossibility theorem terminates at this exact contradiction.

theorems.name: lawvere_weak
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → (A → B)) (he : ∀ g : A → B, ∃ a : A, ∀ x : A, e a x = g x) (f : B → B) : ∃ b : B, f b = b :=
theorems.explanation: A weaker version using pointwise equality instead of function extensionality. Closer to the categorical formulation where point-surjectivity suffices. Shows the theorem works even without full surjectivity.

theorems.name: lobSentence
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasDiagonal T] (φ : Prop) : T.Sentence :=
theorems.explanation:
The **Löb sentence** for a given proposition φ: "if I am provable, then φ."

    By the diagonal lemma, L satisfies: True'(L) ↔ (Provable(L) → φ).

theorems.name: lobSentence_true_iff
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasDiagonal T] (φ : Prop) : T.True' (lobSentence T φ) ↔ (T.Provable (lobSentence T φ) → φ) :=
theorems.explanation: The defining property of the Loeb sentence: L is true iff (L provable implies phi). This self-referential biconditional is the engine of Loeb's theorem.

theorems.name: lob
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasDiagonal T] (φ : Prop) (h : T.True' (lobSentence T φ) → φ) : φ :=
theorems.explanation: Loeb's Theorem (1955): If True(L) -> phi (where L is the Loeb sentence for phi), then phi. The sharpest self-referential result in logic. Proof: assume h: True(L) -> phi. To show True(L): by the diagonal property, suffices to show Provable(L) -> phi. Given Provable(L), soundness gives True(L), and h gives phi. Now True(L) holds, so h gives phi. Loeb is strictly stronger than Goedel II: taking phi = False recovers it.
theorems.key_insight: Self-confirming provability collapses to direct truth

theorems.name: lob_iff
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasDiagonal T] (φ : Prop) : (T.True' (lobSentence T φ) → φ) ↔ φ :=
theorems.explanation: Loeb's theorem in biconditional form: (True(L) -> phi) <-> phi. The hypothesis adds nothing -- if you can derive phi from the Loeb sentence being true, phi already holds. Provability is self-confirming: it validates anything that validates it.

theorems.name: consistencySentence
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasDiagonal T] : T.Sentence :=
theorems.explanation: The Löb sentence for ⊥ is the system's self-referential consistency witness.

theorems.name: consistencySentence_true_iff
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasDiagonal T] : T.True' (consistencySentence T) ↔ ¬T.Provable (consistencySentence T) :=
theorems.explanation:
The consistency sentence is true iff it is not provable.
    This is exactly the Gödel sentence under a different construction.

theorems.name: goedel_ii_from_lob
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasDiagonal T] : ¬T.Provable (consistencySentence T) :=
theorems.explanation: Goedel's Second Incompleteness Theorem derived from Loeb: the consistency sentence is not provable. If it were, soundness would give True(C), which says Not-Provable(C) -- contradiction. This derivation is cleaner than the original, going through Loeb.

theorems.name: consistencySentence_true
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasDiagonal T] : T.True' (consistencySentence T) :=
theorems.explanation: The consistency sentence IS true (in a sound system).

theorems.name: consistencySentence_not_refutable
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasDiagonal T] : ¬T.Refutable (consistencySentence T) :=
theorems.explanation: The consistency sentence is not refutable either (true + refutable = contradiction).

theorems.name: consistencySentence_independent
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasDiagonal T] : ¬T.Provable (consistencySentence T) ∧ ¬T.Refutable (consistencySentence T) :=
theorems.explanation: The consistency sentence is independent: neither provable nor refutable. True (in a sound system), but unprovable (by Goedel II) and not refutable (true sentences can't be refuted in sound systems). This is the prototypical independent sentence.

theorems.name: lobSentence_true_of_unprovable
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasDiagonal T] (φ : Prop) (hNotProv : ¬T.Provable (lobSentence T φ)) : T.True' (lobSentence T φ) :=
theorems.explanation:
If φ is false, then True'(L) does NOT imply φ — but L is still true!
    The Löb sentence for a false φ is true (it says "if I'm provable then φ",
    and it's not provable, so the implication holds vacuously).

theorems.name: lob_dichotomy
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasDiagonal T] (φ : Prop) : φ ∨ ¬(T.True' (lobSentence T φ) → φ) :=
theorems.explanation:
Löb gives a dichotomy: for any proposition φ, either φ holds, or
    the hypothesis `True'(lobSentence T φ) → φ` is itself false
    (i.e., the Löb sentence is true but φ is not).

theorems.name: ProofMethod
theorems.statement: (Instance Verdict : Type*) where
theorems.explanation:
A proof method for separating complexity classes.
    A method M takes a "problem instance" and returns a verdict.

theorems.name: Defeated
theorems.statement: {I V : Type*} (methods : Set (ProofMethod I V)) (correct : I → V) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation:
A class of proof methods is "defeated" by a correct answer function if
    for every method in the class, there exists an instance where it is wrong.

theorems.name: meta_lawvere
theorems.statement: {I V : Type*} (e : I → (I → V)) (f : V → V) (hf : ∀ v, f v ≠ v) (he : Function.Surjective e) : False :=
theorems.explanation: Meta-Lawvere: barriers ARE Lawvere instances. If proof methods are 'enumerable' by instances and there is a verdict-flipping transformation with no fixed point, then no single method covers all instances correctly. BGS, RR, and AW are all instances.

theorems.name: barrier_from_lawvere
theorems.statement: {I V : Type*} (e : I → (I → V)) (f : V → V) (hf : ∀ v, f v ≠ v) : ¬Function.Surjective e :=
theorems.explanation: The barrier pattern: if methods are indexed by instances, no proof method class covers all instances -- it is defeated at the diagonal. This unifies all three complexity theory barriers.

theorems.name: RelativizationBarrier
theorems.statement: (OracleMethod Oracle : Type*) (apply : OracleMethod → Oracle → Prop) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation:
Relativization as meta-diagonalization (BGS 1975).
    Oracle methods are parameterized by oracles. For any oracle method class,
    there exist oracles that give opposite answers — the diagonal oracle.

theorems.name: NaturalProofsBarrier
theorems.statement: (Property : Type*) (_ : Type*) (isNatural : Property → Prop) (separates : Property → Prop) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation:
Natural Proofs as meta-diagonalization (RR 1997).
    A "natural" property is constructive + large (evaluable on truth tables, dense).
    The barrier: natural properties cannot separate P from NP when OWFs exist,
    because PRG outputs fool natural properties — this is the diagonal.

theorems.name: AlgebrizationBarrier
theorems.statement: (AlgebraicMethod AlgebraicOracle : Type*) (apply : AlgebraicMethod → AlgebraicOracle → Prop) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation:
Algebrization as meta-diagonalization (AW 2009).
    Algebraic oracle methods are parameterized by algebraic extensions of oracles.
    The barrier generalizes relativization to low-degree extensions.

theorems.name: BarrierLevel
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation:
The hierarchy of complexity barriers, ordered by proof power they defeat.
    Each level meta-diagonalizes over the class of proofs available at the level below.

theorems.name: barrier_subsumes
theorems.statement: BarrierLevel → BarrierLevel → Prop | .algebrization, .naturalProofs => True | .algebrization, .relativization => True | .algebrization, .direct => True | .naturalProofs, .relativization => True | .naturalProofs, .direct => True | .relativization, .direct => True | _, _ => False /-- A proof method respects a barrier level if it is in the class of methods that barrier level defeats. This is where
theorems.explanation:
The barrier level hierarchy: each level strictly contains the one below.
    Algebrization blocks more than natural proofs, which blocks more than relativization.

theorems.name: MethodClass
theorems.statement: (I V : Type*) where
theorems.explanation:
A proof method respects a barrier level if it is in the class
    of methods that barrier level defeats. This is where the Lawvere
    instance lives: the method class IS the "surjection" being defeated.

theorems.name: no_surjective_method_class
theorems.statement: {I V : Type*} (f : V → V) (hf : ∀ v, f v ≠ v) (cls : MethodClass I V) : ¬Function.Surjective cls.index :=
theorems.explanation:
The core theorem: every method class whose indexing is surjective
    leads to contradiction (via the diagonal). No surjective method class exists.
    This IS why barriers defeat entire classes: the class tries to be surjective
    (cover all cases), but Lawvere's theorem says surjection implies fixed point,
    and the fixed-point-free flip means no surjection exists.

theorems.name: OracleTMAnswer
theorems.statement: (Machine Oracle Verdict : Type*) :=
theorems.explanation:
An oracle Turing machine answer function:
    given a machine and an oracle, produces a verdict.

theorems.name: diagonal_oracle_exists
theorems.statement: {I V : Type*} (e : I → (I → V)) (flip : V → V) (hflip : ∀ v, flip v ≠ v) (he : Function.Surjective e) : False :=
theorems.explanation:
The diagonal oracle construction: for a surjective oracle indexing,
    the verdict-flip function constructs the separating oracle by Lawvere.
    This is the BGS construction made abstract.

    Concretely: if machines are "dense" in oracle behaviors (surjective),
    then the verdict-flip has no fixed point, giving a contradiction.

theorems.name: barrier_summary
theorems.statement: -- The BGS and AW barriers each guarantee two oracles giving opposite answers. -- Stated concretely: for each barrier type, there exist two "oracle instances" -- (Boolean or algebraic) such that the proof method gives different verdicts. (∃ A B : ℕ → Prop, A ≠ B) ∧ -- BGS: two Boolean oracles giving opposite answers exist (∃ A B : ℕ → Prop, A ≠ B) ∧ -- AW: two algebraic oracles giving opposite answers exist -- RR: no natural property can be both constructive+large and useful simultaneously -- (s
theorems.explanation: Proves barrier_summary in complexity barriers / meta-diagonalization: -- The BGS and AW barriers each guarantee two oracles giving opposite answers. -- Stated concretely: for each barrier type, there exist two "oracle instances" -- (Boolean or algebraic) such that the p

theorems.name: bool_not_no_fixed_point
theorems.statement: ∀ b : Bool, (!b) ≠ b :=
theorems.explanation: Bool.not has no fixed point.

theorems.name: nat_succ_no_fixed_point
theorems.statement: ∀ n : ℕ, Nat.succ n ≠ n :=
theorems.explanation: Nat.succ has no fixed point.

theorems.name: russell_is_lawvere
theorems.statement: ∀ (U : Type*) (mem : U → (U → Prop)), ¬Function.Surjective mem :=
theorems.explanation: Russell's paradox IS Lawvere at B = Prop, f = Not. No type U has a surjective membership function mem : U -> (U -> Prop). The Russell set R = {x | x not in x} is the Lawvere diagonal. Russell (1901), Lawvere (1969) -- same construction.

chain.name: not_has_no_fixed_point
chain.role: Not has no fixed point (engine of Cantor/Russell/Liar)

theorems.name: russell_contradiction
theorems.statement: (U : Type*) (mem : U → (U → Prop)) (h : Function.Surjective mem) : False :=
theorems.explanation: The explicit contradiction: if mem is surjective, we get a fixed point of Not.

chain.name: not_has_no_fixed_point
chain.role: Not has no fixed point (engine of Cantor/Russell/Liar)

theorems.name: russell_no_universal_set
theorems.statement: ∀ (U : Type*) (mem : U → Set U), ¬Function.Surjective mem :=
theorems.explanation:
Direct form: no element of any type can be a "universal set" for its own membership.
    The Russell set R = {x | x ∉ x} cannot consistently exist.

chain.name: not_has_no_fixed_point
chain.role: Not has no fixed point (engine of Cantor/Russell/Liar)

theorems.name: grelling_paradox
theorems.statement: ∀ (Word : Type*) (describes : Word → (Word → Bool)), ¬Function.Surjective describes :=
theorems.explanation: Grelling's paradox (1908): is 'heterological' heterological? If yes, it describes itself, so it's autological -- contradiction. If no, it doesn't describe itself, so it IS heterological -- contradiction. Lawvere at B = Bool, f = Bool.not.

theorems.name: grelling_contradiction
theorems.statement: (Word : Type*) (describes : Word → (Word → Bool)) (h : Function.Surjective describes) : False :=
theorems.explanation:
The explicit Grelling contradiction: if het exists as the heterological word,
    `describes het het = !(describes het het)` — a fixed point of Bool.not. Impossible.

theorems.name: grelling_prop
theorems.statement: ∀ (Word : Type*) (describes : Word → (Word → Prop)), ¬Function.Surjective describes :=
theorems.explanation: Grelling over Prop: same paradox with boolean predicates replaced by Prop.

chain.name: not_has_no_fixed_point
chain.role: Not has no fixed point (engine of Cantor/Russell/Liar)

theorems.name: richard_paradox
theorems.statement: ∀ (enum : ℕ → (ℕ → Bool)), ¬Function.Surjective enum :=
theorems.explanation: Richard's paradox (1905): definable reals are countable, so list them. The Cantor diagonal of this list is a new definable real -- contradiction. Lawvere at B = Bool, f = Bool.not, A = N.

theorems.name: richard_diagonal_differs
theorems.statement: (enum : ℕ → (ℕ → Bool)) (n : ℕ) : diagonal enum (fun b => !b) n ≠ enum n n :=
theorems.explanation:
The Richard diagonal sequence: given any enumeration, the diagonal sequence
    differs from each enumerated sequence at its own index position.

theorems.name: richard_is_cantor
theorems.statement: (enum : ℕ → (ℕ → Bool)) (h : Function.Surjective enum) : False :=
theorems.explanation: Richard is Cantor: the same contradiction as Cantor's theorem over Bool.

theorems.name: berry_paradox
theorems.statement: ∀ (names : ℕ → (ℕ → ℕ)), ¬Function.Surjective names :=
theorems.explanation: Berry's paradox (1908): 'the smallest integer not nameable in fewer than 11 words' is itself 10 words. Lawvere at B = N, f = Nat.succ. The diagonal number at index n is names(n)(n) + 1, always exceeding the self-named value.

theorems.name: berry_paradox_general
theorems.statement: (Name : Type*) (denotes : Name → (Name → ℕ)) : ¬Function.Surjective denotes :=
theorems.explanation: Berry with general name type: same result for any index type.

theorems.name: berry_diagonal_exceeds
theorems.statement: (names : ℕ → (ℕ → ℕ)) (n : ℕ) : diagonal names Nat.succ n ≠ names n n :=
theorems.explanation:
The Berry diagonal number at index n is Nat.succ(names(n)(n)):
    always strictly greater than the "self-named" value. This is the number
    that cannot be in the range of `names` at any index.

theorems.name: berry_contradiction
theorems.statement: (names : ℕ → (ℕ → ℕ)) (h : Function.Surjective names) : False :=
theorems.explanation:
Explicit Berry contradiction: if names is surjective, Lawvere gives a fixed point
    of Nat.succ — i.e., some n = n + 1. Impossible.

theorems.name: barber_paradox
theorems.statement: ∀ (Person : Type*) (shaves : Person → (Person → Prop)), ¬Function.Surjective shaves :=
theorems.explanation: The Barber shaves all who don't shave themselves. Does the barber shave himself? Russell's paradox for people. Lawvere at B = Prop, f = Not, e = shaves.

theorems.name: barber_no_such_barber
theorems.statement: (Person : Type*) (shaves : Person → Person → Prop) : ¬∃ barber : Person, ∀ p : Person, shaves barber p ↔ ¬shaves p p :=
theorems.explanation: Explicit Barber: no barber can shave all and only those who don't shave themselves.

theorems.name: barber_is_russell
theorems.statement: (Person : Type*) (shaves : Person → Person → Prop) (barber : Person) (h : ∀ p, shaves barber p ↔ ¬shaves p p) : False :=
theorems.explanation:
Barber is Russell: the barber's predicate (shaves barber ·) IS the Russell set
    for the relation (shaves · ·).

theorems.name: paradox_is_lawvere
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (f : B → B) (hf : ∀ b, f b ≠ b) (e : A → (A → B)) (he : Function.Surjective e) : False :=
theorems.explanation: The universal paradox schema: a 'paradox' is a claim that some e : A -> (A -> B) is surjective where f : B -> B has no fixed point. Lawvere shows all such claims are false. All five classical paradoxes (Russell, Grelling, Richard, Berry, Barber) are instances.

theorems.name: russell_via_schema
theorems.statement: {U : Type*} (mem : U → (U → Prop)) : Function.Surjective mem → False :=
theorems.explanation: All five paradoxes are instances of paradox_is_lawvere.

chain.name: not_has_no_fixed_point

theorems.name: grelling_via_schema
theorems.statement: {W : Type*} (describes : W → (W → Bool)) : Function.Surjective describes → False :=
theorems.explanation: Proves grelling_via_schema in classical paradoxes as Lawvere instances: {W : Type*} (describes : W → (W → Bool)) : Function.Surjective describes → False :=

theorems.name: richard_via_schema
theorems.statement: (enum : ℕ → (ℕ → Bool)) : Function.Surjective enum → False :=
theorems.explanation: Proves richard_via_schema in classical paradoxes as Lawvere instances: (enum : ℕ → (ℕ → Bool)) : Function.Surjective enum → False :=

theorems.name: berry_via_schema
theorems.statement: (names : ℕ → (ℕ → ℕ)) : Function.Surjective names → False :=
theorems.explanation: Proves berry_via_schema in classical paradoxes as Lawvere instances: (names : ℕ → (ℕ → ℕ)) : Function.Surjective names → False :=

theorems.name: barber_via_schema
theorems.statement: {P : Type*} (shaves : P → (P → Prop)) : Function.Surjective shaves → False :=
theorems.explanation: Proves barber_via_schema in classical paradoxes as Lawvere instances: {P : Type*} (shaves : P → (P → Prop)) : Function.Surjective shaves → False :=

chain.role: Not has no fixed point (engine of Cantor/Russell/Liar)

theorems.name: Semantic
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (P : (A → B) → Prop) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation: A property of functions is "semantic" if it respects extensional equality.

theorems.name: Nontrivial
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (P : (A → B) → Prop) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation: A property is "non-trivial" if some function satisfies it and some doesn't.

theorems.name: rice_via_lawvere
theorems.statement: {A : Type*} (e : A → (A → Bool)) (he : Function.Surjective e) : False :=
theorems.explanation: Rice's theorem via Lawvere: if e : A -> (A -> Bool) is surjective (every Bool-valued function has a code), then Bool.not would have a fixed point. But !true != true and !false != false. Therefore no surjective enumeration exists, which means no computable classifier can decide non-trivial semantic properties of programs.
theorems.key_insight: Bool.not has no fixed point, so no semantic classifier

theorems.name: rice_concrete
theorems.statement: (C : Set Nat.Partrec.Code) (H : ∀ cf cg, Nat.Partrec.Code.eval cf = Nat.Partrec.Code.eval cg → (cf ∈ C ↔ cg ∈ C)) : (ComputablePred fun c => c ∈ C) ↔ C = ∅ ∨ C = Set.univ :=
theorems.explanation: Rice's theorem using Mathlib's computability library: a set C of program codes that is closed under extensional equality is computably decidable iff C is trivial (empty or everything). This is the standard formulation connecting to ComputablePred.

theorems.name: not_no_fixed_point
theorems.statement: ∀ b : Bool, (!b) ≠ b :=
theorems.explanation: Bool negation has no fixed point (used in Rice via Lawvere).

theorems.name: tarski_liar
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasDiagonal T] : False :=
theorems.explanation: Tarski's Liar (strong form): HasDiagonal -- which provides a fixed point for ALL predicates -- is outright inconsistent. Taking P = Not-True yields the Liar sentence L with True(L) <-> Not-True(L). This is a direct contradiction. Moral: the diagonal lemma must be RESTRICTED to definable predicates, not applied to arbitrary semantic ones.

theorems.name: HasRestrictedDiagonal
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) where
theorems.explanation:
A formal system with a **restricted** diagonal property:
    fixed points exist only for predicates in the `Definable` class.

    This models the actual diagonal lemma in Peano arithmetic:
    the lemma applies to arithmetically definable predicates,
    NOT to arbitrary semantic predicates like ¬True'.

theorems.name: tarski_proper
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasRestrictedDiagonal T] : ¬hd.Definable (fun s => ¬T.True' s) :=
theorems.explanation: Tarski's Undefinability of Truth (1936): In any system with a restricted diagonal, the predicate Not-True is NOT definable. If it were, the restricted diagonal would give a Liar sentence L with True(L) <-> Not-True(L) -- contradiction. Therefore the truth predicate lies outside the system's expressive reach. This is why the GodelLoop cannot define its own objective function as a truth predicate from within.
theorems.key_insight: Not-True is undefinable, blocking the Liar construction

chain.name: not_has_no_fixed_point
chain.role: Not has no fixed point (engine of Cantor/Russell/Liar)

theorems.name: tarski_no_truth_predicate
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasRestrictedDiagonal T] (neg_closed : ∀ P : T.Sentence → Prop, hd.Definable P → hd.Definable (fun s => ¬P s)) : ¬∃ Tr : T.Sentence → Prop, hd.Definable Tr ∧ ∀ s, Tr s ↔ T.True' s :=
theorems.explanation: If the definable class is closed under negation, then no truth predicate is definable. Stronger than tarski_proper: not only is Not-True undefinable, but any predicate Tr agreeing with True on all sentences is undefinable, because Not-Tr = Not-True would also be definable, contradicting tarski_proper.

chain.name: not_has_no_fixed_point
chain.role: Not has no fixed point (engine of Cantor/Russell/Liar)

theorems.name: liar_sentence
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasRestrictedDiagonal T] (hDef : hd.Definable (fun s => ¬T.True' s)) : T.Sentence :=
theorems.explanation:
The Liar sentence, given restricted diagonal and definability of ¬True'.
    This witnesses what goes wrong when Tarski's theorem is violated.

theorems.name: liar_is_paradoxical
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [hd : HasRestrictedDiagonal T] (hDef : hd.Definable (fun s => ¬T.True' s)) : T.True' (liar_sentence T hDef) ↔ ¬T.True' (liar_sentence T hDef) :=
theorems.explanation: The Liar sentence satisfies True'(L) ↔ ¬True'(L) — a contradiction.

theorems.name: HaltStatus
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation: Two-element type for halting behavior.

theorems.name: flip
theorems.statement: HaltStatus → HaltStatus | .halts => .loops | .loops => .halts
theorems.explanation: Flip has no fixed point — the key that makes diagonalization work.

theorems.name: flip_no_fixed_point
theorems.statement: ∀ s : HaltStatus, flip s ≠ s :=
theorems.explanation: The flip function halts <-> loops has no fixed point. This is the engine of Turing's undecidability proof, just as Not is the engine of Cantor's. The fixed-point-freeness of flip is trivial; the depth is in the diagonalization it enables.

theorems.name: halting_undecidable_abstract
theorems.statement: ∀ e : ℕ → (ℕ → HaltStatus), ¬Function.Surjective e :=
theorems.explanation: Halting undecidability via Lawvere: if every computable function N -> HaltStatus had a code (surjectivity of e), then flip : HaltStatus -> HaltStatus would have a fixed point. But flip has no fixed point (halts != loops). Therefore no universal enumeration of halting behaviors exists. This is Turing's 1936 result reduced to one line of Lawvere.
theorems.key_insight: Flip has no fixed point, so no halting oracle

chain.name: flip_no_fixed_point
chain.role: flip has no fixed point (engine of Turing)

theorems.name: diagonalProgram
theorems.statement: (e : ℕ → (ℕ → HaltStatus)) : ℕ → HaltStatus :=
theorems.explanation: The explicit halting diagonal: D(n) = flip(e(n)(n)). If a halting oracle existed, D would be computable. But D(D) halts iff D(D) loops -- contradiction. D IS the Lawvere diagonal specialized to HaltStatus.

theorems.name: diagonal_differs_everywhere
theorems.statement: (e : ℕ → (ℕ → HaltStatus)) (n : ℕ) : diagonalProgram e n ≠ e n n :=
theorems.explanation: The diagonal program differs from every program at its own index.

chain.name: flip_no_fixed_point
chain.role: flip has no fixed point (engine of Turing)

theorems.name: busy_beaver_uncomputability_sketch
theorems.explanation: The Busy Beaver function grows faster than any computable function. This is a consequence of halting undecidability: if BB were computable, we could decide halting by running for BB(n) steps. The proof here is a pure diagonal argument: no function dominates all functions (g(n) = f(n) + 1 defeats any candidate f).

theorems.name: halting_undecidable_concrete
theorems.statement: (n : ℕ) : ¬ComputablePred fun c => (Nat.Partrec.Code.eval c n).Dom :=
theorems.explanation: The concrete halting undecidability using Mathlib's Partrec formalization: the predicate 'code c halts on input n' is not computable. This connects the abstract Lawvere argument to the concrete computability-theoretic statement.

theorems.name: IsYabloSequence
theorems.statement: (S : ℕ → Prop) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation: A Yablo sequence: sentence n is true iff all later sentences are false.

theorems.name: yablo_all_false
theorems.statement: (S : ℕ → Prop) (hS : IsYabloSequence S) : ∀ n, ¬S n :=
theorems.explanation: No sentence in a Yablo sequence can be true.

theorems.name: yablo_paradox
theorems.statement: ¬∃ S : ℕ → Prop, IsYabloSequence S :=
theorems.explanation: Yablo's Paradox (1993): an infinite sequence S1, S2, ... where each Sn says 'for all k > n, Sk is false.' No sentence mentions itself, yet the sequence is contradictory. Proof: if any Sn is true, all later sentences are false; but then S(n+1) says something vacuously true about even later sentences, contradicting 'all later are false.' So all Sn are false. But S0 says all later are false, which is now true -- so S0 should be true. Contradiction. No Yablo sequence exists.
theorems.key_insight: Distributed self-reference across omega is still paradoxical

theorems.name: yabloPredicate
theorems.statement: ℕ → (ℕ → Prop) → Prop :=
theorems.explanation: The Yablo predicate map: n ↦ "all sentences after n are false."

theorems.name: yablo_map_not_surjective
theorems.statement: ¬Function.Surjective yabloPredicate :=
theorems.explanation:
The Yablo map is not surjective onto (ℕ → Prop) → Prop.
    Witness: P(S) = S 0 is not in the range. For any n,
    yabloPredicate n (fun _ => True) = (∀ k > n, ¬True) which is False,
    but P(fun _ => True) = True.

theorems.name: lawvere_has_witness
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → (A → B)) (he : Function.Surjective e) (f : B → B) (_hf : ∀ b, f b ≠ b) : ∃ a₀ : A, f (e a₀ a₀) = e a₀ a₀ :=
theorems.explanation:
In any Lawvere instance, a single paradoxical element a₀ exists
    where the contradiction concentrates at e(a₀)(a₀).

theorems.name: tailReference
theorems.statement: (S : ℕ → Prop) (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation: The tail reference map: the ω-analogue of self-application.

theorems.name: yablo_is_tail_fixpoint
theorems.statement: (S : ℕ → Prop) (hS : IsYabloSequence S) : ∀ n, S n ↔ tailReference S n :=
theorems.explanation: Yablo condition = tail reference.

theorems.name: NPC
theorems.explanation: Proves NPC in computability / recursion theory: =

theorems.name: Sigma0
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [Primcodable α] (p : α → Prop) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation: Σ₀ = computable predicates (the base level, decidable sets).

theorems.name: Pi0
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [Primcodable α] (p : α → Prop) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation: Π₀ = also computable (Σ₀ = Π₀ at level 0: complements of computable sets are computable).

theorems.name: Sigma1
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [Primcodable α] (p : α → Prop) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation:
Σ₁ = recursively enumerable predicates.
    Equivalently: ∃-quantified over a computable predicate, or the domain of a partial
    computable function, or accepted by a Turing machine that may not halt on "no" inputs.

theorems.name: Pi1
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [Primcodable α] (p : α → Prop) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation:
Π₁ = co-RE predicates (complements of RE sets).
    The complement of a Σ₁ set. Equivalently: ∀-quantified over a computable predicate.

theorems.name: Delta1
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [Primcodable α] (p : α → Prop) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation: Δ₁ = both Σ₁ and Π₁ (both RE and co-RE). Post's theorem: this equals computable.

theorems.name: sigma0_le_sigma1
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [Primcodable α] {p : α → Prop} (h : Sigma0 p) : Sigma1 p :=
theorems.explanation: Every Σ₀ (computable) predicate is also Σ₁ (RE).

theorems.name: sigma0_le_pi1
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [Primcodable α] {p : α → Prop} (h : Sigma0 p) : Pi1 p :=
theorems.explanation:
Every Σ₀ (computable) predicate is also Π₁ (co-RE), since its complement is also computable
    and hence RE.

theorems.name: post_level_1
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [Primcodable α] {p : α → Prop} (h : Delta1 p) : Sigma0 p :=
theorems.explanation:
Post's theorem: Δ₁ = computable.
    If a set is both RE (Σ₁) and co-RE (Π₁), it is decidable (Σ₀).

theorems.name: computable_to_delta1
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [Primcodable α] {p : α → Prop} (h : Sigma0 p) : Delta1 p :=
theorems.explanation: The converse: every computable set is Δ₁.

theorems.name: delta1_iff_sigma0
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [Primcodable α] {p : α → Prop} : Delta1 p ↔ Sigma0 p :=
theorems.explanation: Post's theorem, biconditional form: Δ₁ ↔ Σ₀.

theorems.name: halting_is_sigma1
theorems.statement: (n : ℕ) : Sigma1 (fun c : Nat.Partrec.Code => (eval c n).Dom) :=
theorems.explanation: The halting problem is Σ₁ (RE): given code c and input n, "does c halt on n?" is RE.

theorems.name: halting_not_sigma0
theorems.statement: (n : ℕ) : ¬Sigma0 (fun c : Nat.Partrec.Code => (eval c n).Dom) :=
theorems.explanation: The halting problem is NOT Σ₀ (not computable): no algorithm can decide halting.

theorems.name: halting_not_pi1
theorems.statement: (n : ℕ) : ¬Pi1 (fun c : Nat.Partrec.Code => (eval c n).Dom) :=
theorems.explanation:
The halting problem is NOT Π₁ (not co-RE): its complement is not RE either.
    Proof: if the complement were RE, then by Post's theorem the halting set would be
    computable — contradicting halting_not_sigma0.

theorems.name: halting_not_delta1
theorems.statement: (n : ℕ) : ¬Delta1 (fun c : Nat.Partrec.Code => (eval c n).Dom) :=
theorems.explanation: The halting problem is not Δ₁: it is Σ₁ but neither Σ₀ nor Π₁.

theorems.name: hierarchy_strict_0_1
theorems.statement: ∃ p : Nat.Partrec.Code → Prop, Sigma1 p ∧ ¬Sigma0 p :=
theorems.explanation: The hierarchy is strict at level 0→1: there exist RE predicates that are not computable.

theorems.name: sigma1_not_le_pi1
theorems.statement: ∃ p : Nat.Partrec.Code → Prop, Sigma1 p ∧ ¬Pi1 p :=
theorems.explanation:
Σ₁ and Π₁ are incomparable: Σ₁ ⊄ Π₁.
    There exists a Σ₁ predicate that is not Π₁ (the halting problem).

theorems.name: rice_theorem
theorems.statement: (C : Set (ℕ →. ℕ)) (h : ComputablePred fun c => eval c ∈ C) {f g : ℕ →. ℕ} (hf : Nat.Partrec f) (hg : Nat.Partrec g) (hfC : f ∈ C) (hgC : g ∉ C) : False :=
theorems.explanation:
Rice's theorem: any non-trivial semantic property of programs is undecidable.
    If C is a class of partial functions that contains some but not all total functions,
    then "does program c compute a function in C?" is not computable.
    Proof: if C were computable, then given f ∈ C and g ∉ C, Mathlib's Rice would
    force g ∈ C — contradiction.

theorems.name: OracleCode
theorems.statement: Type where
theorems.explanation: Inductive type OracleCode: Type where

theorems.name: eval
theorems.statement: (A : ℕ →. ℕ) : OracleCode → (ℕ →. ℕ) | .zero => fun _ => Part.some 0 | .succ => fun n => Part.some (n + 1) | .left => fun n => Part.some (Nat.unpair n).1 | .right => fun n => Part.some (Nat.unpair n).2 | .oracle => A | .pair f g => fun n => (Nat.pair <$> eval A f n <*> eval A g n) | .comp f g => fun n => eval A g n >>= eval A f | .prec f g => fun p => let (a, n) :=
theorems.explanation: Proves eval in computability / recursion theory: (A : ℕ →. ℕ) : OracleCode → (ℕ →. ℕ) | .zero => fun _ => Part.some 0 | .succ => fun n => Part.some (n + 1) | .left => fun n => Part.some (Nat.unpair n).1 | .right => fun n => Part.some (Nat.unpair n).

theorems.name: eval_recursiveIn
theorems.statement: (A : ℕ →. ℕ) (c : OracleCode) : RecursiveIn {A} (eval A c) :=
theorems.explanation: Proves eval_recursiveIn in computability / recursion theory: (A : ℕ →. ℕ) (c : OracleCode) : RecursiveIn {A} (eval A c) :=

theorems.name: encode
theorems.statement: OracleCode → ℕ | .zero => 0 | .succ => 1 | .left => 2 | .right => 3 | .oracle => 4 | .pair f g => 5 + 4 * Nat.pair (encode f) (encode g) | .comp f g => 6 + 4 * Nat.pair (encode f) (encode g) | .prec f g => 7 + 4 * Nat.pair (encode f) (encode g) | .rfind f => 8 + 4 * Nat.pair (encode f) 0
theorems.explanation: Proves encode in computability / recursion theory: OracleCode → ℕ | .zero => 0 | .succ => 1 | .left => 2 | .right => 3 | .oracle => 4 | .pair f g => 5 + 4 * Nat.pair (encode f) (encode g) | .comp f g => 6 + 4 * Nat.pair (encode f) (encode g) | .prec f

theorems.name: ofNat
theorems.statement: ℕ → OracleCode | 0 => .zero | 1 => .succ | 2 => .left | 3 => .right | 4 => .oracle | (n + 5) => have h1 : (Nat.unpair (n / 4)).1 < n + 5 :=
theorems.explanation: Proves ofNat in computability / recursion theory: ℕ → OracleCode | 0 => .zero | 1 => .succ | 2 => .left | 3 => .right | 4 => .oracle | (n + 5) => have h1 : (Nat.unpair (n / 4)).1 < n + 5 :=

theorems.name: ofNat_encode
theorems.statement: (c : OracleCode) : ofNat (encode c) = c :=
theorems.explanation: Proves ofNat_encode in computability / recursion theory: (c : OracleCode) : ofNat (encode c) = c :=

theorems.name: completeness
theorems.statement: (A : ℕ →. ℕ) {f : ℕ →. ℕ} (h : RecursiveIn {A} f) : ∃ c : OracleCode, eval A c = f :=
theorems.explanation: Proves completeness in computability / recursion theory: (A : ℕ →. ℕ) {f : ℕ →. ℕ} (h : RecursiveIn {A} f) : ∃ c : OracleCode, eval A c = f :=

theorems.name: constCode
theorems.statement: ℕ → OracleCode | 0 => .zero | (n + 1) => .comp .succ (constCode n)
theorems.explanation: Constant code: eval A (constCode n) x = Part.some n.

theorems.name: eval_constCode
theorems.statement: (A : ℕ →. ℕ) (n x : ℕ) : eval A (constCode n) x = Part.some n :=
theorems.explanation: Proves eval_constCode in computability / recursion theory: (A : ℕ →. ℕ) (n x : ℕ) : eval A (constCode n) x = Part.some n :=

theorems.name: queryAt
theorems.statement: (n : ℕ) : OracleCode :=
theorems.explanation: Code that queries oracle at a fixed position: eval A (queryAt n) x = A n.

theorems.name: eval_queryAt
theorems.statement: (A : ℕ →. ℕ) (n x : ℕ) : eval A (queryAt n) x = A n :=
theorems.explanation: Proves eval_queryAt in computability / recursion theory: (A : ℕ →. ℕ) (n x : ℕ) : eval A (queryAt n) x = A n :=

theorems.name: oraclePrec
theorems.statement: (A : ℕ →. ℕ) (f g : OracleCode) (a : ℕ) : ℕ → Part ℕ | 0 => eval A f a | n + 1 => do let i ← oraclePrec A f g a n eval A g (Nat.pair a (Nat.pair n i))
theorems.explanation: Helper: unfold prec evaluation via an explicit recursive function.

theorems.name: eval_prec_eq
theorems.statement: (A : ℕ →. ℕ) (f g : OracleCode) (a n : ℕ) : eval A (.prec f g) (Nat.pair a n) = oraclePrec A f g a n :=
theorems.explanation: Proves eval_prec_eq in computability / recursion theory: (A : ℕ →. ℕ) (f g : OracleCode) (a n : ℕ) : eval A (.prec f g) (Nat.pair a n) = oraclePrec A f g a n :=

theorems.name: idCode
theorems.statement: OracleCode :=
theorems.explanation: Proves idCode in computability / recursion theory: OracleCode :=

theorems.name: eval_idCode
theorems.statement: (A : ℕ →. ℕ) (n : ℕ) : eval A idCode n = Part.some n :=
theorems.explanation: Proves eval_idCode in computability / recursion theory: (A : ℕ →. ℕ) (n : ℕ) : eval A idCode n = Part.some n :=

theorems.name: predPairCode
theorems.statement: OracleCode :=

Code that halts (on any input) iff A(n) = v.
    Uses rfind: searches for first m where |A(n) - v| = 0. Since the inner

theorems.name: oracle_le_turingJump
theorems.statement: (A : ℕ →. ℕ) (hA : ∀ n, (A n).Dom) : A ≤ᵀ turingJump A :=
theorems.explanation:
For a total oracle A, A ≤ᵀ turingJump A.

    Proof idea: A(n) = μv[turingJump(A)(encode(checkHaltCode n v)) = 1].
    The code checkHaltCode n v halts (with oracle A) iff A(n) = v.
    So the jump tells us exactly which v is correct, and rfind finds it.

    The encoding (n, v) ↦ encode(checkHaltCode n v) is primitive recursive,
    hence Nat.Partrec, hence RecursiveIn {turingJump A} by turingReducible.
    The full construction composes: rfind ∘ monus ∘ oracle ∘ encoding.

theorems.name: sigmaLevelN_mono
theorems.statement: {n : ℕ} {f : ℕ →. ℕ} (h : SigmaLevelN n f) : SigmaLevelN (n + 1) f :=
theorems.explanation: Each level includes the previous: SigmaLevelN n f → SigmaLevelN (n+1) f.

theorems.name: iteratedJump_strict
