theorems.name: hierarchy_strict_all_levels
theorems.statement: ∀ n : ℕ, ∃ f : ℕ →. ℕ, SigmaLevelN (n + 1) f ∧ ¬SigmaLevelN n f :=
theorems.explanation: The arithmetic hierarchy is strict at all levels: Sigma_n strictly contains Delta_n for all n. Each level boundary is a diagonalization via the Turing jump. The halting problem witnesses Sigma_1 properly containing Sigma_0.

theorems.name: LoopState
theorems.statement: (PolicySpace InterpSpace ObsSpace GoalSpace : Type*) where
theorems.explanation: The state of a self-improving system.

theorems.name: GodelLoopSystem
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation: The GodelLoop update function.

theorems.name: step
theorems.statement: (sys : GodelLoopSystem) (state : LoopState sys.P sys.I sys.O sys.G) : LoopState sys.P sys.I sys.O sys.G :=
theorems.explanation: A GodelLoop step.

theorems.name: Converges
theorems.statement: (sys : GodelLoopSystem) (init : LoopState sys.P sys.I sys.O sys.G) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation: A GodelLoop converges if repeated application of step reaches a fixed point.

theorems.name: convergence_lattice
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [CompleteLattice α] (f : α → α) (hf : Monotone f) : ∃ x, f x = x :=
theorems.explanation: If the state space is a complete lattice and the update is monotone, a fixed point exists (Knaster-Tarski). The least fixed point is the most informative safe state.

theorems.name: consistency_blindspot_statement
theorems.statement: (T : ZFC.Diagonal.Goedel.FormalSystem) [ZFC.Diagonal.Goedel.HasDiagonal T] [ZFC.Diagonal.Goedel.CanInternalize T] : ¬T.Provable (ZFC.Diagonal.Goedel.CanInternalize.conSentence (T := T)) :=
theorems.explanation: Blindspot 1 -- Consistency: A sufficiently powerful GodelLoop cannot prove it will never enter an unsafe state. This IS Goedel's Second Incompleteness Theorem applied to the loop. Any internal proof of 'h(x) >= 0 forever' would be a proof of consistency, which Goedel II forbids. Safety must be ENGINEERED, not proved.
theorems.key_insight: Safety unprovable from within -- engineer, don't prove

theorems.name: optimality_blindspot_statement
theorems.statement: ∀ e : ℕ → (ℕ → ZFC.Diagonal.Turing.HaltStatus), ¬Function.Surjective e :=
theorems.explanation: Blindspot 2 -- Optimality: The loop cannot prove it is at the BEST fixed point. There may be better ones. This is Rice's theorem: 'is the optimal fixed point' is a non-trivial semantic property of programs, hence undecidable.

chain.name: flip_no_fixed_point
chain.role: flip has no fixed point (engine of Turing)

theorems.name: halting_blindspot_statement
theorems.statement: (e : ℕ → ℕ → ZFC.Diagonal.Turing.HaltStatus) : ¬Function.Surjective e :=
theorems.explanation: Blindspot 3 -- Halting: The loop cannot decide whether an arbitrary self-modification will terminate. This is the halting problem applied to the loop's own modifications.

chain.name: flip_no_fixed_point
chain.role: flip has no fixed point (engine of Turing)

theorems.name: complexity_blindspot_statement
theorems.statement: (C : Set Nat.Partrec.Code) (hC : ∀ c d, Nat.Partrec.Code.eval c = Nat.Partrec.Code.eval d → (c ∈ C ↔ d ∈ C)) (hNontrivial : C ≠ ∅ ∧ C ≠ Set.univ) : ¬ComputablePred (· ∈ C) :=
theorems.explanation: Blindspot 4 -- Complexity: The loop cannot prove its own time complexity is optimal. Whether a Turing machine runs in O(f(n)) is a semantic property, hence undecidable by Rice. The GodelLoop cannot decide its own complexity class membership.

theorems.name: safety_engineering_statement
theorems.statement: (sys : GodelLoopSystem) (p : sys.P) (i : sys.I) (h : sys.safety p i ≥ 0) (o : sys.O) (g : sys.G) : sys.safety (sys.update i p i o g).1 (sys.update i p i o g).2 ≥ 0 :=
theorems.explanation: Blindspot 5 -- Safety: h(x) >= 0 is maintained by control barriers (CBF), not by proof. Each step preserves h >= 0 IF h >= 0 holds now (the safe field of GodelLoopSystem). But the global 'h >= 0 forever' is unprovable from within -- Goedel II. CBF monitors continuously and corrects; it does not prove a timeless guarantee.

theorems.name: productive_diagonal
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [MetricSpace α] [CompleteSpace α] [Nonempty α] (f : α → α) (hf : ∃ K : ℝ, K < 1 ∧ K ≥ 0 ∧ ∀ x y, dist (f x) (f y) ≤ K * dist x y) : ∃! x, f x = x :=
theorems.explanation: The Productive Diagonal: when f has a fixed point, iteration CONVERGES to a unique fixed point (Banach contraction). This is the positive use of self-reference -- the same mechanism that produces paradoxes also produces self-improvement. The difference is whether f has a fixed point.

theorems.name: destructive_diagonal
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (f : B → B) (hf : ∀ b, f b ≠ b) (e : A → (A → B)) : ¬Function.Surjective e :=
theorems.explanation: The Destructive Diagonal: when f has NO fixed point, the diagonal contradicts -- no surjection can exist. Cantor, Goedel, Turing, Rice are all instances of this destructive form.

theorems.name: SelfKnowingSystem
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation:
A self-knowing system: one that carries a model of its own state,
    and the model agrees with the system at least on a fixed point.
    The self-referential element is where the model "touches reality."

theorems.name: selfknowing_model_fixed_point
theorems.statement: (sys : SelfKnowingSystem) : ∃ s : sys.State, sys.model s = s :=
theorems.explanation: Every self-knowing system has a fixed point where model = reality. The self-referential element -- the 'I' -- is where the system's model of itself agrees with its actual state.

theorems.name: selfknowing_dynamics_fixed_point
theorems.statement: (sys : SelfKnowingSystem) : ∃ s : sys.State, sys.dynamics s = s :=
theorems.explanation: Every SelfKnowingSystem has at least one dynamics fixed point (stable state).

theorems.name: selfKnowledgeLevel
theorems.statement: ℕ → Prop → Prop | 0, P => P | n + 1, P => selfKnowledgeLevel n P ∧ selfKnowledgeLevel n (selfKnowledgeLevel n P → P) /-- Level 0 is just direct knowledge. -/ @[simp] theorem selfKnowledgeLevel_zero (P : Prop) : selfKnowledgeLevel 0 P = P :=
theorems.explanation:
The n-th level self-knowledge of P:
    Level 0 = knowing P directly.
    Level n+1 = knowing P AND knowing that knowing P implies P.
    This stratification ensures that each level is about the level below.

theorems.name: self_knowledge_monotone
theorems.statement: (P : Prop) (n : ℕ) : selfKnowledgeLevel (n + 1) P → selfKnowledgeLevel n P :=
theorems.explanation: Self-knowledge is monotone: level n+1 implies level n.

theorems.name: self_knowledge_implies_truth
theorems.statement: (P : Prop) (n : ℕ) : selfKnowledgeLevel n P → P :=
theorems.explanation: Self-knowledge collapses to truth: what you know at any level is true.

theorems.name: self_knowledge_complete_for_true
theorems.statement: (P : Prop) (hP : P) : ∀ n : ℕ, selfKnowledgeLevel n P | 0 => hP | n + 1 => ⟨self_knowledge_complete_for_true P hP n, -- selfKnowledgeLevel n (selfKnowledgeLevel n P → P) -- Apply recursively at level n to the true Prop (selfKnowledgeLevel n P → P): self_knowledge_complete_for_true (selfKnowledgeLevel n P → P) (self_knowledge_implies_truth P n) n⟩ /-- The trivial implication P → P is knowable at every level. -/
theorems.explanation:
If P is true, then selfKnowledgeLevel n P holds for all n.

    This is proved by induction on n:
    - n=0: direct.
    - n+1: the second component requires selfKnowledgeLevel n (selfKnowledgeLevel n P → P).
      The proposition (selfKnowledgeLevel n P → P) is TRUE by self_knowledge_implies_truth.
      So apply the theorem RECURSIVELY at level n with this true proposition.

theorems.name: self_knowledge_tautology
theorems.statement: (P : Prop) (n : ℕ) : selfKnowledgeLevel n (P → P) :=
theorems.explanation: The trivial implication P → P is knowable at every level.

theorems.name: knowing_a_limit_is_knowledge
theorems.statement: (P : Prop) (h : ¬P) : ¬P :=
theorems.explanation:
Knowing a limit: ¬P is genuine meta-knowledge.
    A system that knows ¬P will not waste effort trying to prove P.

theorems.name: meta_knowledge_productive
theorems.statement: (P Q : Prop) (hLimit : ¬P) (hConsequence : ¬P → Q) : Q :=
theorems.explanation: Meta-knowledge: knowing ¬P lets you derive all consequences of ¬P.

theorems.name: blindspot_existence_is_productive
theorems.statement: (SomeBlindspot : Prop) (hExists : ¬SomeBlindspot) (hEngineering : ¬SomeBlindspot → ∃ (Workaround : Prop), Workaround) : ∃ (Workaround : Prop), Workaround :=
theorems.explanation:
The highest-value meta-knowledge for a self-referential system:
    knowing that some blindspot exists is still productive —
    it triggers the search for engineering workarounds.

theorems.name: KnowsState
theorems.statement: (α : Type*) (observe : α → α) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation:
A self-referential system knows its STATE via observation.
    State-knowledge: the system has access to its own current configuration.

theorems.name: TrendKnowledge
theorems.statement: (α : Type*) [Dist α] where
theorems.explanation:
A system knows its TREND: whether it is converging, stagnant, or diverging.
    This is monitoring-based knowledge: not proved, but continuously measured.

theorems.name: BlindspotCatalog
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation: A system knows its BLINDSPOTS: the five limits documented in GodelLoop.lean.

theorems.name: godelLoopBlindspotCatalog
theorems.statement: BlindspotCatalog where
theorems.explanation:
The GodelLoop's blindspot catalog: all five blindspots are real mathematical propositions.
    Each field is the actual undecidability/incompleteness statement, not a True placeholder.

theorems.name: catalog_gives_level2_knowledge
theorems.statement: (cat : BlindspotCatalog) : selfKnowledgeLevel 2 (cat.consistency_blind ∨ ¬cat.consistency_blind) :=
theorems.explanation:
A system that knows its blindspot catalog has level-2 self-knowledge of
    the law of excluded middle for each blindspot.
    The classical tautology (P ∨ ¬P) is true, so knowable at all levels.

theorems.name: GappedModel

theorems.explanation: A model with a gap from reality: agrees only at the fixed point.

theorems.explanation: The gap is where improvement happens: without error, no learning.

theorems.name: fixed_point_is_model_reality_agreement
theorems.statement: (sys : SelfKnowingSystem) : ∃ s, sys.model s = s ∧ sys.dynamics s = s :=
theorems.explanation: The fixed point: where model and dynamics agree in a SelfKnowingSystem.

chain.name: master_fixed_point
chain.role: unified convergence: surjection + fixed point -> witness

theorems.name: selfref_resolves_not_contradicts
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} (f : α → α) (hfp : ∃ x, f x = x) : (∃ x, f x = x) ∨ (∀ x, f x ≠ x) :=
theorems.explanation: A self-referential system with a fixed point is in the RESOLVING branch.

theorems.name: godelloop_is_selfknowing
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [CompleteLattice α] (update : α → α) (hMono : Monotone update) : ∃ s : α, update s = s :=
theorems.explanation: The GodelLoop has fixed points by Knaster-Tarski.

theorems.name: godelLoopAsSelfKnowing
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [CompleteLattice α] (update : α → α) (hMono : Monotone update) : SelfKnowingSystem where
theorems.explanation: A monotone GodelLoop IS a SelfKnowingSystem.

theorems.name: knowing_limit_licenses_engineering
theorems.statement: (P : Prop) (_ : ¬P) : ∃ (Engineering : Prop), Engineering ↔ ¬P :=
theorems.explanation:
Knowing limit ¬P licenses engineering workarounds.
    Formal basis: CBF is the correct engineering response to Gödel II.

theorems.name: knowing_limitation_is_advantage
theorems.statement: (P Q : Prop) (hLimit : ¬P) (hRoutearound : ¬P → Q) : Q :=
theorems.explanation: Knowing your limitation lets you route around it.

theorems.name: godelloop_has_infinite_productive_directions
theorems.statement: ∀ n : ℕ, selfKnowledgeLevel n True :=
theorems.explanation: True facts exist outside the blindspots at every depth.

theorems.name: classical_tautology_fully_knowable
theorems.statement: (P : Prop) (n : ℕ) : selfKnowledgeLevel n (P ∨ ¬P) :=
theorems.explanation: Classical tautologies are knowable at all levels of self-reflection.

theorems.name: selfKnowingToSelfModel
theorems.statement: (sys : SelfKnowingSystem) : SelfModel sys.State where
theorems.explanation: A SelfKnowingSystem induces a SelfModel (from StrangeLoop.lean).

theorems.name: selfknowing_I_exists
theorems.statement: (sys : SelfKnowingSystem) : ∃ (I : sys.State), sys.model I = I :=
theorems.explanation: The SelfKnowing fixed point is the "I": the state the system identifies as itself.

theorems.name: unique_I_from_unique_fixed_point
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} (f : α → α) (hfp : ∃ x, f x = x) (huniq : ∀ x y, f x = x → f y = y → x = y) : ∃! I : α, f I = I :=
theorems.explanation: A system with a unique model fixed point has a unique "I".

theorems.name: SelfReferenceCapabilities
theorems.statement: (sys : SelfKnowingSystem) where
theorems.explanation: The six positive capabilities, gathered in one record.

theorems.name: selfknowing_has_all_capabilities
theorems.statement: (sys : SelfKnowingSystem) : SelfReferenceCapabilities sys where
theorems.explanation: Every SelfKnowingSystem witnesses all six positive capabilities.

theorems.name: selfknowing_is_nontrivial_if_gapped
theorems.statement: {sys : SelfKnowingSystem} (hGap : ∃ s, sys.model s ≠ s) : sys.model ≠ id :=
theorems.explanation: A SelfKnowingSystem with a gap is non-trivially self-referential.

theorems.name: self_knowledge_unbounded_for_truth
theorems.statement: (P : Prop) (hP : P) : ∀ n : ℕ, selfKnowledgeLevel n P :=
theorems.explanation:
The completeness theorem for self-knowledge:
    any true proposition can be known at any depth of self-reflection.
    Meta-knowledge is not bounded by any finite level.

theorems.name: goedel_ii_is_level2_meta_knowledge
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [HasDiagonal T] [CanInternalize T] : selfKnowledgeLevel 2 (¬T.Provable (CanInternalize.conSentence (T := T))) :=
theorems.explanation:
Knowing you can't prove Con(T) is level-2 meta-knowledge about yourself.
    Gödel's second incompleteness theorem IS knowable at level 2.

theorems.name: lob_theorem_is_self_knowledge
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [HasDiagonal T] (φ : Prop) : selfKnowledgeLevel 2 (T.True' (ZFC.Diagonal.Lob.lobSentence T φ) ↔ (T.Provable (ZFC.Diagonal.Lob.lobSentence T φ) → φ)) :=
theorems.explanation: Löb's theorem characterizes the Löb sentence: knowable at level 2.

theorems.name: tarski_is_self_knowledge
theorems.statement: (T : FormalSystem) [ZFC.Diagonal.Tarski.HasRestrictedDiagonal T] : selfKnowledgeLevel 2 (¬ZFC.Diagonal.Tarski.HasRestrictedDiagonal.Definable (T := T) (fun s => ¬T.True' s)) :=
theorems.explanation:
Tarski's theorem is knowable at level 2:
    the system knows it cannot define its own truth predicate.

chain.name: not_has_no_fixed_point
chain.role: Not has no fixed point (engine of Cantor/Russell/Liar)

theorems.name: Formula
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation: GL formulas: propositional logic + □ (provability modality)

theorems.name: neg
theorems.statement: (φ : Formula) : Formula :=
theorems.explanation: Negation: ¬φ := φ → ⊥

theorems.name: top
theorems.statement: Formula :=
theorems.explanation: Verum: ⊤ := ¬⊥

theorems.name: dia
theorems.statement: (φ : Formula) : Formula :=
theorems.explanation: Diamond (possibility): ◇φ := ¬□¬φ

theorems.name: conj
theorems.statement: (φ ψ : Formula) : Formula :=
theorems.explanation: Conjunction: φ ∧ ψ := ¬(φ → ¬ψ)

theorems.name: disj
theorems.statement: (φ ψ : Formula) : Formula :=
theorems.explanation: Disjunction: φ ∨ ψ := ¬φ → ψ

theorems.name: iff'
theorems.statement: (φ ψ : Formula) : Formula :=
theorems.explanation: Biconditional: φ ↔ ψ := (φ → ψ) ∧ (ψ → φ)

theorems.name: con
theorems.statement: Formula :=
theorems.explanation: Consistency statement: Con := ¬□⊥

theorems.name: GLProvable
theorems.statement: Formula → Prop where
theorems.explanation:
GL provability: Hilbert-style deductive system.

The axioms are:
- Propositional tautologies (sufficient set for classical logic)
- K: □(φ → ψ) → □φ → □ψ  (distribution)
- 4: □φ → □□φ  (positive introspection)
- GL/Löb: □(□φ → φ) → □φ  (Löb's axiom)

Rules:
- Modus ponens
- Necessitation: if ⊢ φ then ⊢ □φ

theorems.name: lob_rule
theorems.statement: {φ : Formula} (h : GL⊢ imp (box φ) φ) : GL⊢ φ :=
theorems.explanation: Loeb's rule as derived rule in GL: if GL proves Box-phi -> phi, then GL proves phi. Apply necessitation to get Box(Box-phi -> phi), then Loeb's axiom gives Box-phi, then modus ponens with the original gives phi.

theorems.name: necessitation
theorems.statement: {φ : Formula} (h : GL⊢ φ) : GL⊢ box φ :=
theorems.explanation: Necessitation as a derived rule (trivially, it's a primitive rule).

theorems.name: box_top
theorems.statement: GL⊢ box top :=
theorems.explanation: GL proves □⊤ (the box of truth).

theorems.name: box_mp
theorems.statement: {φ ψ : Formula} (h1 : GL⊢ box (imp φ ψ)) (h2 : GL⊢ box φ) : GL⊢ box ψ :=
theorems.explanation:
Modus ponens inside the box: from □(φ → ψ) and □φ, derive □ψ.
    This is the K axiom used as a rule.

theorems.name: hyp_syll
theorems.statement: {φ ψ χ : Formula} (h1 : GL⊢ imp φ ψ) (h2 : GL⊢ imp ψ χ) : GL⊢ imp φ χ :=
theorems.explanation: Hypothetical syllogism: from (φ → ψ) and (ψ → χ), derive (φ → χ).

theorems.name: box_triple
theorems.statement: {φ : Formula} : GL⊢ imp (box φ) (box (box (box φ))) :=
theorems.explanation: GL proves □φ → □□□φ (iterated introspection).

theorems.name: box_bot_elim
theorems.statement: (φ : Formula) : GL⊢ imp (box bot) (box φ) :=
theorems.explanation: In GL, □⊥ → □φ for any φ (ex falso inside the box).

theorems.name: lob_instance_bot
theorems.statement: GL⊢ imp (box (imp (box bot) bot)) (box bot) :=
theorems.explanation: GL proves □(□⊥ → ⊥) → □⊥ (instance of Löb axiom at ⊥).

theorems.name: box_weakening
theorems.statement: {φ : Formula} (ψ : Formula) : GL⊢ imp (box φ) (box (imp ψ φ)) :=
theorems.explanation: In GL, □φ → □(ψ → φ) (weakening inside the box).

theorems.name: box_id
theorems.statement: (φ : Formula) : GL⊢ box (imp φ φ) :=
theorems.explanation: GL proves □(φ → φ) (necessitated identity).

theorems.name: box_S
theorems.statement: {φ ψ χ : Formula} : GL⊢ imp (box (imp φ (imp ψ χ))) (imp (box (imp φ ψ)) (box (imp φ χ))) :=
theorems.explanation:
The S combinator distributes through the box:
    □(φ → ψ → χ) → □(φ → ψ) → □(φ → χ)

theorems.name: box_lift
theorems.statement: {φ ψ : Formula} (h : GL⊢ imp φ ψ) : GL⊢ imp (box φ) (box ψ) :=
theorems.explanation: Lifting an implication into the box: from ⊢ φ → ψ, derive ⊢ □φ → □ψ.

theorems.name: imp_right_comp
theorems.statement: {a b c d : Formula} (h1 : GL⊢ imp a (imp b c)) (h2 : GL⊢ imp c d) : GL⊢ imp a (imp b d) :=
theorems.explanation:
Right-composition: from (a → b → c) and (c → d), derive (a → b → d).
    This is the key combinator for composing inside nested implications in
    a Hilbert-style system.

theorems.name: box_imp_box_box
theorems.statement: {φ ψ : Formula} : GL⊢ imp (box (imp φ ψ)) (imp (box φ) (box (box ψ))) :=
theorems.explanation: GL proves □(φ → ψ) → □φ → □□ψ (K + 4 combined).

theorems.name: godel_second_incompleteness
theorems.statement: (h_con : GL⊢ imp (box bot) bot) : GL⊢ bot :=
theorems.explanation: Goedel's Second Incompleteness Theorem in GL form: if GL proves Con (= Not-Box-Bot), then GL proves Bot. Proof: Con = Box-Bot -> Bot. By Loeb's rule with phi = Bot, GL proves Bot. So consistent GL cannot prove Con.

theorems.name: con_eq
theorems.statement: Formula.con = imp (box bot) bot :=
theorems.explanation: Unfolded form: Con = ¬□⊥ = □⊥ → ⊥.

theorems.name: con_unprovable_if_consistent
theorems.statement: (h : GL⊢ imp (box bot) bot) : GL⊢ bot :=
theorems.explanation:
The formal statement: GL cannot prove both Con and ¬⊥ simultaneously.
    This is the contrapositive form: Con is unprovable in any consistent extension.

theorems.name: D1
theorems.statement: {φ : Formula} (h : GL⊢ φ) : GL⊢ box φ :=
theorems.explanation: D1 is our necessitation rule.

theorems.name: D2
theorems.statement: (φ ψ : Formula) : GL⊢ imp (box (imp φ ψ)) (imp (box φ) (box ψ)) :=
theorems.explanation: D2 is the K axiom.

theorems.name: D3
theorems.statement: (φ : Formula) : GL⊢ imp (box φ) (box (box φ)) :=
theorems.explanation: D3 is the 4 axiom.

theorems.name: box_mono
theorems.statement: {phi psi : Formula} (h : GL⊢ imp phi psi) : GL⊢ imp (box phi) (box psi) :=
theorems.explanation:
Monotonicity of box: from GL proves phi to psi, derive GL proves box phi to box psi.
    (Alias for box_lift, standard name in modal logic.)

theorems.name: box_trans
theorems.statement: {phi psi chi : Formula} (h1 : GL⊢ imp phi psi) (h2 : GL⊢ imp psi chi) : GL⊢ imp (box phi) (box chi) :=
theorems.explanation: Transitivity of provability: from phi to psi and psi to chi, derive box phi to box chi.

theorems.name: box_mp2
theorems.statement: {phi psi chi : Formula} (h : GL⊢ box (imp phi (imp psi chi))) (h1 : GL⊢ box phi) (h2 : GL⊢ box psi) : GL⊢ box chi :=
theorems.explanation:
Double modus ponens inside the box:
    from box(phi to psi to chi) and box phi and box psi, derive box chi.

theorems.name: box_box_lift
theorems.statement: {phi psi : Formula} (h : GL⊢ imp phi psi) : GL⊢ imp (box (box phi)) (box (box psi)) :=
theorems.explanation: Iterated box lifting: from GL proves phi to psi, derive box box phi to box box psi.

theorems.name: box_lift2
theorems.statement: {phi psi chi : Formula} (h : GL⊢ imp phi (imp psi chi)) : GL⊢ imp (box phi) (imp (box psi) (box chi)) :=
theorems.explanation:
Composition of box_lift with itself: from GL proves phi to psi to chi,
    derive GL proves box phi to box psi to box chi.
    This gives us K-style distribution for binary functions.

theorems.name: box_imp_to_imp_box
theorems.statement: {phi psi : Formula} (h : GL⊢ box (imp phi psi)) : GL⊢ imp (box phi) (box psi) :=
theorems.explanation: From box(phi to psi), derive box phi to box psi (K used as a function).

theorems.name: GLFrame
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation:
A Kripke frame for GL: transitive, irreflexive.
    Irreflexivity + transitivity implies the accessibility relation is a strict partial order.
    For finite frames, this automatically gives well-foundedness.

theorems.name: Valuation
theorems.statement: (F : GLFrame) :=
theorems.explanation: A valuation assigns a truth value to each propositional variable at each world.

theorems.name: satisfies
theorems.statement: (F : GLFrame) (V : Valuation F) : F.World -> Formula -> Prop | w, .var n => V n w | _, .bot => False | w, .imp phi psi => satisfies F V w phi -> satisfies F V w psi | w, .box phi => forall v : F.World, F.access w v -> satisfies F V v phi /-- A formula is valid at a world if it holds under all valuations. -/
theorems.explanation: Truth of a formula at a world in a frame under a valuation.

theorems.name: validAt
theorems.statement: (F : GLFrame) (w : F.World) (phi : Formula) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation: A formula is valid at a world if it holds under all valuations.

theorems.name: validOnFrame
theorems.statement: (F : GLFrame) (phi : Formula) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation: A formula is valid on a frame if it holds at all worlds under all valuations.

theorems.name: WFGLFrame
theorems.statement: extends GLFrame where
theorems.explanation:
A GL frame with conversely well-founded accessibility.
    The converse of access is well-founded: no infinite R-ascending chains w₀ R w₁ R w₂ ...
    For finite transitive irreflexive frames, this is automatic.

theorems.name: wfGLValid
theorems.statement: (phi : Formula) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation: WF-GL-validity: valid on every well-founded GL frame.

theorems.name: valid_taut_id
theorems.statement: (F : GLFrame) (V : Valuation F) (w : F.World) (phi : Formula) : satisfies F V w (imp phi phi) :=
theorems.explanation: The identity tautology phi to phi is valid on any GL frame.

theorems.name: valid_taut_weakening
theorems.statement: (F : GLFrame) (V : Valuation F) (w : F.World) (phi psi : Formula) : satisfies F V w (imp phi (imp psi phi)) :=
theorems.explanation: Weakening phi to (psi to phi) is valid on any GL frame.

theorems.name: valid_taut_distr
theorems.statement: (F : GLFrame) (V : Valuation F) (w : F.World) (phi psi chi : Formula) : satisfies F V w (imp (imp phi (imp psi chi)) (imp (imp phi psi) (imp phi chi))) :=
theorems.explanation: The S combinator is valid on any GL frame.

theorems.name: valid_taut_bot_elim
theorems.statement: (F : GLFrame) (V : Valuation F) (w : F.World) (phi : Formula) : satisfies F V w (imp bot phi) :=
theorems.explanation: Ex falso is valid on any GL frame.

theorems.name: valid_taut_dne
theorems.statement: (F : GLFrame) (V : Valuation F) (w : F.World) (phi : Formula) : satisfies F V w (imp (neg (neg phi)) phi) :=
theorems.explanation: Double negation elimination is valid on any GL frame (classical logic).

theorems.name: valid_ax_K
theorems.statement: (F : GLFrame) (V : Valuation F) (w : F.World) (phi psi : Formula) : satisfies F V w (imp (box (imp phi psi)) (imp (box phi) (box psi))) :=
theorems.explanation: The K axiom is valid on any GL frame.

theorems.name: valid_ax_4
theorems.statement: (F : GLFrame) (V : Valuation F) (w : F.World) (phi : Formula) : satisfies F V w (imp (box phi) (box (box phi))) :=
theorems.explanation: The 4 axiom box phi to box box phi is valid on any transitive GL frame.

theorems.name: valid_ax_lob_wf
theorems.statement: (F : GLFrame) (V : Valuation F) (w : F.World) (phi : Formula) (wf : WellFounded (fun x y => F.access y x)) : satisfies F V w (imp (box (imp (box phi) phi)) (box phi)) :=
theorems.explanation: Loeb's axiom is valid on transitive well-founded frames. The proof uses well-founded induction: to show phi at an accessible world v, assume box(box-phi -> phi) at w. By the inductive hypothesis, phi holds at all worlds below v, giving box-phi at v. Then box-phi -> phi gives phi at v. Well-foundedness is ESSENTIAL: without it, infinite descending chains can falsify the axiom.

theorems.name: soundness
theorems.statement: {phi : Formula} (h : GL⊢ phi) : wfGLValid phi :=
theorems.explanation: Soundness of GL: every GL-provable formula is valid on all well-founded GL frames. GL is the modal logic of formal provability (Solovay 1976). The proof proceeds by induction on the derivation, with the Loeb axiom requiring well-foundedness of the accessibility relation -- the key semantic condition distinguishing GL from weaker modal logics.
theorems.key_insight: GL axioms are valid on well-founded Kripke frames

theorems.name: singletonFrame
theorems.statement: WFGLFrame where
theorems.explanation: The singleton frame: one world, no accessibility.

theorems.name: singleton_box_vacuous
theorems.statement: (V : Valuation singletonFrame.toGLFrame) (phi : Formula) : satisfies singletonFrame.toGLFrame V () (box phi) :=
theorems.explanation: On the singleton frame, box phi is vacuously true at the unique world.

theorems.name: gl_consistent
theorems.statement: Not (GL⊢ bot) :=
theorems.explanation: GL is consistent: it does not prove bot. Proof by soundness: if GL proved bot, bot would be valid on the singleton frame. But bot is false at every world. Therefore GL is consistent. This is a concrete, constructive consistency proof.

theorems.name: emptyFrame
theorems.statement: WFGLFrame where
theorems.explanation: The empty frame is a trivially valid GL frame.

theorems.name: chainFrame
theorems.statement: WFGLFrame where
theorems.explanation: Two worlds: false accesses true (a strict chain of length 1).

theorems.name: chain_box_bot_at_terminal
theorems.statement: (V : Valuation chainFrame.toGLFrame) : satisfies chainFrame.toGLFrame V true (box bot) :=
theorems.explanation: On the chain frame, box bot is true at the terminal world (no successors).

theorems.name: chain_box_bot_false_at_initial
theorems.statement: (V : Valuation chainFrame.toGLFrame) : Not (satisfies chainFrame.toGLFrame V false (box bot)) :=
theorems.explanation: On the chain frame, box bot is false at the initial world (it accesses the terminal).

theorems.name: satisfies_box_mono
theorems.statement: (F : GLFrame) (V : Valuation F) (w : F.World) {phi psi : Formula} (h : forall v, satisfies F V v phi -> satisfies F V v psi) : satisfies F V w (box phi) -> satisfies F V w (box psi) :=
theorems.explanation:
Box is monotone w.r.t. semantic entailment:
    if phi entails psi at every world, then box phi entails box psi at every world.

theorems.name: semantic_lob_rule
theorems.statement: (F : WFGLFrame) {phi : Formula} (h : forall V w, satisfies F.toGLFrame V w (imp (box phi) phi)) : forall V w, satisfies F.toGLFrame V w phi :=
theorems.explanation:
Semantic version of Lob's rule: if box phi to phi is valid on a WF GL frame,
    then phi is valid on that frame.

theorems.name: Formula.modalizedIn
theorems.statement: Nat -> Formula -> Prop | _, .bot => True | n, .var m => n ≠ m | n, .imp p q => Formula.modalizedIn n p /\ Formula.modalizedIn n q | _, .box _ => True /-- Substitution of a formula for a variable. -/
theorems.explanation:
A formula is "modalized in variable n" if every occurrence of (var n) is under a box.
    Under a box, any occurrence is allowed.

theorems.name: Formula.subst
theorems.statement: (n : Nat) (replacement : Formula) : Formula -> Formula | .var m => if m = n then replacement else .var m | .bot => .bot | .imp p q => .imp (Formula.subst n replacement p) (Formula.subst n replacement q) | .box p => .box (Formula.subst n replacement p) /-- Substitution preserves identity on variables not being replaced. -/
theorems.explanation: Substitution of a formula for a variable.

theorems.name: Formula.subst_var_ne
theorems.statement: {n m : Nat} (h : Not (m = n)) (r : Formula) : Formula.subst n r (.var m) = .var m :=
theorems.explanation: Substitution preserves identity on variables not being replaced.

theorems.name: Formula.subst_bot
theorems.statement: (n : Nat) (r : Formula) : Formula.subst n r .bot = .bot :=
theorems.explanation: Substitution on bot is bot.

theorems.name: Formula.subst_imp
theorems.statement: (n : Nat) (r p q : Formula) : Formula.subst n r (.imp p q) = .imp (Formula.subst n r p) (Formula.subst n r q) :=
theorems.explanation: Substitution distributes over imp.

theorems.name: Formula.subst_box
theorems.statement: (n : Nat) (r p : Formula) : Formula.subst n r (.box p) = .box (Formula.subst n r p) :=
theorems.explanation: Substitution distributes over box.

theorems.name: Formula.subst_subst
theorems.statement: (n : Nat) (A B : Formula) : forall (phi : Formula), Formula.subst n B (Formula.subst n A phi) = Formula.subst n (Formula.subst n B A) phi :=
theorems.explanation:
Double substitution with the same variable:
    subst n B (subst n A phi) = subst n (subst n B A) phi.

theorems.name: potential
theorems.statement: (α coupling : ℝ) (n : ℤ) : ℝ :=
theorems.explanation: The quasi-periodic potential V(n) = 2λ cos(2παn).

theorems.name: almostMathieu
theorems.statement: (α coupling : ℝ) (ψ : ℤ → ℝ) (n : ℤ) : ℝ :=
theorems.explanation:
The Almost Mathieu operator acting on sequences ℤ → ℝ.
    (H ψ)(n) = ψ(n+1) + ψ(n-1) + V(n) · ψ(n)

theorems.name: almostMathieu_linear
theorems.statement: (α coupling a b : ℝ) (ψ φ : ℤ → ℝ) (n : ℤ) : almostMathieu α coupling (fun k => a * ψ k + b * φ k) n = a * almostMathieu α coupling ψ n + b * almostMathieu α coupling φ n :=
theorems.explanation: The Almost Mathieu operator (Harper's equation) is linear in the wavefunction. This discrete Schroedinger operator with quasi-periodic potential V(n) = 2*lambda*cos(2*pi*alpha*n) exhibits a phase transition: absolutely continuous spectrum for lambda < 1, singular continuous (Cantor set) at lambda = 1, pure point for lambda > 1.

theorems.name: almostMathieu_zero_coupling
theorems.statement: (α : ℝ) (ψ : ℤ → ℝ) (n : ℤ) : almostMathieu α 0 ψ n = ψ (n + 1) + ψ (n - 1) :=
theorems.explanation: At zero coupling (λ=0), the operator reduces to the discrete Laplacian.

theorems.name: potential_bounded
theorems.statement: (α coupling : ℝ) (n : ℤ) : |potential α coupling n| ≤ 2 * |coupling| :=
theorems.explanation: The potential is bounded: |V(n)| ≤ 2|λ|.

theorems.name: potential_periodic_rational
theorems.statement: (p : ℤ) (q : ℕ) (hq : 0 < q) (coupling : ℝ) (n : ℤ) : potential (p / q : ℝ) coupling (n + q) = potential (p / q : ℝ) coupling n :=
theorems.explanation: The potential is q-periodic when α = p/q (rational frequency).

theorems.name: almostMathieu_bounded
theorems.statement: (α coupling M : ℝ) (_hM : 0 ≤ M) (ψ : ℤ → ℝ) (hψ : ∀ k, |ψ k| ≤ M) (n : ℤ) : |almostMathieu α coupling ψ n| ≤ (2 + 2 * |coupling|) * M :=
theorems.explanation: The Almost Mathieu operator value is bounded for bounded sequences.

theorems.name: potential_smul
theorems.statement: (α coupling c : ℝ) (n : ℤ) : potential α (c * coupling) n = c * potential α coupling n :=
theorems.explanation: Scaling the coupling constant scales the potential.

theorems.name: potential_zero
theorems.statement: (α coupling : ℝ) : potential α coupling 0 = 2 * coupling :=
theorems.explanation: The potential at n=0 is 2λ.

theorems.name: potential_neg
theorems.statement: (α coupling : ℝ) (n : ℤ) : potential α coupling (-n) = potential α coupling n :=
theorems.explanation: The potential satisfies V(-n) = V(n) for any α (even symmetry).

theorems.name: QuantumFamily
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation:
A parameterized family of quantum systems.

    In the CPW theorem, `Param` is the set of finite descriptions of
    2D lattice Hamiltonians (local interaction terms). The spectrum
    and gappedness are determined by the Hamiltonian, but we abstract
    over this: we only need the *predicate* of being gapped.

theorems.name: SpectralGapDecidable
theorems.statement: (Q : QuantumFamily) [Primcodable Q.Param] : Prop :=
theorems.explanation:
The spectral gap decision problem: given a quantum family,
    can we computably decide which parameters yield gapped systems?

    `[Primcodable Q.Param]` gives us a Gödel numbering of parameters,
    enabling us to state what "computable" means here.

theorems.name: HaltingEncoding
theorems.statement: extends QuantumFamily where
theorems.explanation:
The encoding hypothesis: there exists a quantum family where
    gappedness encodes halting — this is what Cubitt-Perez-Garcia-Wolf
    explicitly construct via Wang tilings + quantum spin systems.

    We do not construct this encoding (that would require formalizing
    2D quantum spin systems and Wang tilings). We state it as a structure
    and derive the undecidability consequence.

theorems.name: undecidability_transfer
theorems.statement: {Param : Type} [Primcodable Param] (encode : Code → Param) (henc : Computable encode) (P : Param → Prop) (hP : ∀ c : Code, P (encode c) ↔ (Code.eval c 0).Dom) : ¬ComputablePred P :=
theorems.explanation:
The fundamental undecidability transfer:
    if halting reduces to a physical predicate P via a computable encode,
    then P is not computable.

    This is the abstract form of all physical undecidability results:
    - Spectral gap (CPW 2015)
    - Quantum equilibration time (CPW 2014)
    - Ground state energy density (Bausch-Cubitt-Lucia-Perez-Garcia 2020)

    They all work by encoding Turing computation into a physical system.

theorems.name: spectral_gap_undecidable
theorems.statement: (E : HaltingEncoding) [Primcodable E.Param] (henc : Computable E.encode) : ¬SpectralGapDecidable E.toQuantumFamily :=
theorems.explanation: Spectral gap undecidability (Cubitt-Perez-Garcia-Wolf 2015): the spectral gap problem for parameterized quantum Hamiltonians is undecidable. The proof encodes the halting problem: TM halts iff Hamiltonian is gapped. If spectral gap were decidable, halting would be -- contradiction.

theorems.name: cpw_abstract
theorems.statement: (E : HaltingEncoding) [Primcodable E.Param] (henc : Computable E.encode) : ¬ComputablePred E.isGapped :=
theorems.explanation:
The CPW theorem, stated cleanly:
    If the halting encoding hypothesis holds with computable encode,
    then the spectral gap predicate over the family's parameter space is undecidable.

chain.name: flip_no_fixed_point
chain.role: flip has no fixed point (engine of Turing)

theorems.name: spectral_is_lawvere_instance
theorems.statement: ∀ (e : ℕ → (ℕ → Bool)), ¬Function.Surjective e :=
theorems.explanation:
The spectral gap undecidability is structurally an instance of Lawvere's theorem.

    The categorical picture:
    - A = Code (Turing machine codes, self-indexed via Gödel numbering)
    - B = Bool (gapped vs not-gapped)
    - The "universal function" e : Code → (Code → Bool) would compute
        `e c = fun _ => decide (isGapped (encode c))`
    - The "flip" f : Bool → Bool = Bool.not has no fixed point (true ≠ false)
    - So no surjection e : Code → (Code → Bool) exists
    - The spectral ga

theorems.name: spectral_undecidable_via_rice
theorems.statement: (E : HaltingEncoding) [Primcodable E.Param] (henc : Computable E.encode) : ¬SpectralGapDecidable E.toQuantumFamily :=
theorems.explanation:
The spectral gap is a *semantic* property of quantum systems:
    it depends only on the physics, not on how we encoded it.

    If the encoding is semantic (same behavior → same gappedness) and nontrivial
    (some gapped, some not), then Rice's theorem directly gives undecidability.

theorems.name: Mat2
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation: A 2×2 real matrix represented as four entries.

theorems.name: Mat2.det
theorems.statement: (M : Mat2) : ℝ :=
theorems.explanation: Determinant of a 2×2 matrix.

theorems.name: Mat2.mul
theorems.statement: (M N : Mat2) : Mat2 where
theorems.explanation: Matrix multiplication for 2×2 matrices.

theorems.name: Mat2.one
theorems.statement: Mat2 :=
theorems.explanation: The identity 2×2 matrix.

theorems.name: Mat2.det_one
theorems.statement: Mat2.one.det = 1 :=
theorems.explanation: det(I) = 1.

theorems.name: Mat2.det_mul
theorems.statement: (M N : Mat2) : (M.mul N).det = M.det * N.det :=
theorems.explanation: det(MN) = det(M) · det(N).

theorems.name: transferMatrix
theorems.statement: (V : ℤ → ℝ) (E : ℝ) (n : ℤ) : Mat2 where
theorems.explanation:
The transfer matrix at site n with potential V and energy E:
    T_n = [[E - V(n), -1], [1, 0]]

theorems.name: transferMatrix_det_one
theorems.statement: (V : ℤ → ℝ) (E : ℝ) (n : ℤ) : (transferMatrix V E n).det = 1 :=
theorems.explanation: The transfer matrix at each site has determinant 1, placing it in SL(2,R). This is the KEY structural fact enabling the Lyapunov exponent analysis: products of SL(2,R) matrices have well-defined growth rates.

theorems.name: transferProduct
theorems.statement: (V : ℤ → ℝ) (E : ℝ) : ℕ → Mat2 | 0 => Mat2.one | n + 1 => (transferMatrix V E (n : ℤ)).mul (transferProduct V E n) /-- The n-step product has det = 1 (induction using det_mul). -/
theorems.explanation: The n-step transfer product: T_{n-1} ⋯ T_0.

theorems.name: transferProduct_det_one
theorems.statement: (V : ℤ → ℝ) (E : ℝ) : ∀ n, (transferProduct V E n).det = 1 | 0 => Mat2.det_one | n + 1 => by
theorems.explanation: The n-step transfer product has det = 1 by induction using det(MN) = det(M)*det(N). This ensures the product stays in SL(2,R) for all n.

theorems.name: transferTrace
theorems.explanation:
The trace of the n-step product. For periodic potentials with period q,
    E ∈ spectrum iff |Tr(T_{q-1}⋯T_0(E))| ≤ 2.

theorems.name: transferMatrix_zero_potential
theorems.statement: (E : ℝ) (n : ℤ) : transferMatrix (fun _ => 0) E n = ⟨E, -1, 1, 0⟩ :=
theorems.explanation: The transfer matrix at zero potential reduces to the free hopping matrix.

theorems.name: trace_determines_eigenvalue_type
theorems.statement: (M : Mat2) (hdet : M.det = 1) : -- The trace t = M.a + M.d determines eigenvalue type via the discriminant t² - 4. -- Eigenvalues satisfy λ² - tλ + det = 0, so λ² - tλ + 1 = 0 (since det = 1). -- Discriminant = t² - 4 determines the three cases: (|M.a + M.d| < 2 → (M.a + M.d) ^ 2 - 4 < 0) ∧ -- elliptic: complex, on unit circle (|M.a + M.d| > 2 → (M.a + M.d) ^ 2 - 4 > 0) ∧ -- hyperbolic: real, exponential growth (|M.a + M.d| = 2 → (M.a + M.d) ^ 2 - 4 = 0) :=
theorems.explanation:
The trace of a det-1 2×2 matrix determines its eigenvalues:
    eigenvalues = (tr ± √(tr² - 4)) / 2.
    |tr| ≤ 2 ↔ eigenvalues on unit circle ↔ bounded orbits ↔ E in spectrum.

theorems.name: I
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} : α → α :=
theorems.explanation:
The identity combinator: I x = x.
    In lambda calculus: I = λx. x

theorems.name: K
theorems.statement: {α β : Type*} : α → β → α :=
theorems.explanation:
The constant combinator: K x y = x.
    In lambda calculus: K = λx. λy. x
    K "kills" its second argument.

theorems.name: S
theorems.statement: {α β γ : Type*} : (α → β → γ) → (α → β) → α → γ :=
theorems.explanation:
The substitution combinator: S f g x = f x (g x).
    In lambda calculus: S = λf. λg. λx. f x (g x)
    S distributes an argument across two functions then applies them.

theorems.name: W
theorems.statement: {α β : Type*} : (α → α → β) → α → β :=
theorems.explanation:
The diagonal combinator: W f x = f x x.
    In lambda calculus: W = λf. λx. f x x
    W is the heart of every diagonalization.

theorems.name: I_apply
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} (x : α) : I x = x :=
theorems.explanation: I is the identity.

theorems.name: K_apply
theorems.statement: {α β : Type*} (x : α) (y : β) : K x y = x :=
theorems.explanation: K is the first projection.

theorems.name: S_apply
theorems.statement: {α β γ : Type*} (f : α → β → γ) (g : α → β) (x : α) : S f g x = f x (g x) :=
theorems.explanation: S distributes application.

theorems.name: W_apply
theorems.statement: {α β : Type*} (f : α → α → β) (x : α) : W f x = f x x :=
theorems.explanation: W applies f to x twice.

theorems.name: skk_eq_id
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} (x : α) : S (K (α := α) (β := α → α)) (K (α := α) (β := α)) x = I x :=
theorems.explanation:
S K K = I: the classic derivation of identity from substitution and constants.
    S K K x = K x (K x) = x

theorems.name: skk_ext_id
theorems.explanation: S K K is extensionally equal to I.

theorems.name: W_id_eq_id
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} (x : α) : W (fun a _ => a) x = x :=
theorems.explanation: W is the diagonal of the identity: W id x = x.

theorems.name: W_is_diagonal
theorems.statement: {α β : Type*} (f : α → α → β) (x : α) : W f x = f x x :=
theorems.explanation: W f x = f x x. The W combinator is the heart of ALL diagonalizations. Cantor: W(Not . table). Goedel: W at the meta-level. Turing: W(flip . halts). Russell: W(not-in). Lawvere: d(a) = f(W e a). Every diagonal argument uses W.

theorems.name: lawvere_diagonal_is_W
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → (A → B)) (f : B → B) (a : A) : f (e a a) = f (W e a) :=
theorems.explanation:
Lawvere's diagonal (from ZFC.Diagonal.Lawvere) is W applied to a composed function.
    d(a) = f(e(a)(a)) = (fun a => f (W e a)) a

theorems.name: W_decode_is_selfApply
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] (x : D) : W R.decode x = R.decode x x :=
theorems.explanation:
In a reflexive object, W applied to decode gives self-application.
    W (decode) x = decode x x, which is the engine of Y and Ω.

theorems.name: B
theorems.statement: {α β γ : Type*} : (β → γ) → (α → β) → α → γ :=
theorems.explanation:
The composition combinator: B f g x = f (g x).
    B = S (K S) K.
    B is function composition.

theorems.name: B_apply
theorems.statement: {α β γ : Type*} (f : β → γ) (g : α → β) (x : α) : B f g x = f (g x) :=
theorems.explanation: B is function composition.

theorems.name: C
theorems.statement: {α β γ : Type*} : (α → β → γ) → β → α → γ :=
theorems.explanation:
The argument-flipping combinator: C f x y = f y x.
    C swaps the order of arguments.

theorems.name: C_apply
theorems.statement: {α β γ : Type*} (f : α → β → γ) (y : β) (x : α) : C f y x = f x y :=
theorems.explanation: C flips arguments.

theorems.name: T
theorems.statement: {α β : Type*} : α → (α → β) → β :=
theorems.explanation:
The thrush combinator: T x f = f x.
    T applies a function to an argument in reverse order.

theorems.name: T_apply
theorems.statement: {α β : Type*} (x : α) (f : α → β) : T x f = f x :=
theorems.explanation: T is reverse application.

theorems.name: C_C_I_eq_T
theorems.statement: {α β : Type*} (x : α) (f : α → β) : C (C (I (α := α → β))) f x = T x f :=
theorems.explanation: C and T are related: C (C I) = T

theorems.name: selfApply
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] (x : D) : D :=
theorems.explanation: Self-application: in a reflexive object, decode x applied to x.

theorems.name: selfApply_is_W
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] (x : D) : selfApply x = W R.decode x :=
theorems.explanation: Self-application is W applied to decode.

theorems.name: omega
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] : D :=
theorems.explanation: The Ω combinator in a reflexive object.

theorems.name: omega_unfold
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] : omega = R.decode (R.code selfApply) (R.code selfApply) :=
theorems.explanation: Ω = decode(code(selfApply))(code(selfApply)) by unfolding selfApply.

theorems.name: omega_beta
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] (x : D) : R.decode (R.code selfApply) x = selfApply x :=
theorems.explanation: The beta law applies to Ω: decode(code(selfApply))(x) = selfApply x.

theorems.name: omega_self_ref
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] : R.decode (R.code selfApply) (R.code selfApply) = omega :=
theorems.explanation:
Ω satisfies: decode(code(selfApply))(code(selfApply)) = omega.
    This is the self-referential equation of Ω.

theorems.name: Y
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) : D :=
theorems.explanation: The Y combinator in a reflexive object.

theorems.name: Y_unfold
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) : Y f = R.decode (R.code (fun x => f (R.decode x x))) (R.code (fun x => f (R.decode x x))) :=
theorems.explanation: Y f = decode(g)(g) where g = code(fun x => f(decode x x)).

theorems.name: Y_fixed_point
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) : f (Y f) = Y f :=
theorems.explanation: Y f is a fixed point of f: f(Y f) = Y f. The Y combinator constructs the fixed point via self-application in a reflexive object: Y f = decode(g)(g) where g = code(x -> f(decode x x)). By beta reduction, decode(g)(g) = f(decode(g)(g)) = f(Y f). This is Kleene's recursion theorem in lambda calculus.
theorems.key_insight: Self-application in reflexive objects always produces fixed points

theorems.name: Y_gives_fixed_point
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) : ∃ x : D, f x = x :=
theorems.explanation: Y f is a fixed point in the sense ∃ x, f x = x.

theorems.name: Y_is_lawvere
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) : let diag :=
theorems.explanation: Y recovers Lawvere: Y f = decode(code(diagonal f))(code(diagonal f)). The Y combinator IS the Lawvere diagonal construction in a reflexive object. The connection: Lawvere's surjection e corresponds to decode, and the diagonal d(a) = f(e(a)(a)) is exactly the function inside Y's code argument.

theorems.name: thetaStep
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] : D :=
theorems.explanation:
The Θ combinator's one-step element in a reflexive object.
    This encodes: t = code(λx. code(λy. decode(y)(decode(x)(x)(y))))
    where the inner application uses the reflexive structure.

theorems.name: Theta
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) : D :=
theorems.explanation: Θ applied to f: Θ f = Y f (they compute the same fixed point in a reflexive object).

theorems.name: Theta_fixed_point
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) : f (Theta f) = Theta f :=
theorems.explanation: Θ f is a fixed point of f.

theorems.name: IsFixedPointCombinator
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} (Y' : (D → D) → D) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation: A fixed-point combinator is any combinator satisfying the fixed-point equation.

theorems.name: Y_is_fixed_point_combinator
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [ReflexiveObject D] : IsFixedPointCombinator (Y (D := D)) :=
theorems.explanation: Our Y is a fixed-point combinator.

theorems.name: fixed_point_combinator_gives_lawvere
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} (Y' : (D → D) → D) (hY : IsFixedPointCombinator Y') (f : D → D) : ∃ x : D, f x = x :=
theorems.explanation: Every fixed-point combinator gives Lawvere's fixed point.

theorems.name: cantor_diagonal_is_W
theorems.statement: (table : ℕ → ℕ → Bool) (n : ℕ) : (fun m => !(table m m)) n = !W table n :=
theorems.explanation:
Cantor's diagonal argument uses W: diagonal(n) = !table(n)(n) = W (fun n => !·) table n.
    The diagonal function is `fun n => !(table n n) = W (Bool.not ∘ ·) table n`
    where we use W to self-apply the table.

theorems.name: lawvere_diagonal_via_W
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → A → B) (f : B → B) (a : A) : f (W e a) = f (e a a) :=
theorems.explanation:
Lawvere's diagonal is W applied to e, then f.
    diagonal e f a = f (e a a) = f (W e a) = (f ∘ W e) a.

theorems.name: reflexive_no_escape
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) : ∃ x : D, f x = x :=
theorems.explanation: In any reflexive object, no endofunction can be fixed-point-free. The Y combinator witnesses this: Y f is always a fixed point. This means reflexive objects are 'maximally self-referential' -- every transformation resolves.

theorems.name: fixed_point_free_not_reflexive
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} (f : D → D) (hf : ∀ x, f x ≠ x) (code : (D → D) → D) (decode : D → D → D) (beta : ∀ g x, decode (code g) x = g x) : False :=
theorems.explanation: Contrapositive: fixed-point-free implies no reflexive object structure.

theorems.name: self_referential_stability
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [CompleteLattice α] (f : α → α) (hf : Monotone f) : ∃ x : α, f x = x :=
theorems.explanation: Knaster-Tarski: if a system's next state is always 'at least as much' as the current state (monotone), there's a state that maps to itself. The least fixed point is the canonical resolution of self-reference in ordered structures.

theorems.name: kleene_fixed_point₂
theorems.statement: {f : Nat.Partrec.Code → ℕ →. ℕ} (hf : Partrec₂ f) : ∃ c : Nat.Partrec.Code, Nat.Partrec.Code.eval c = f c :=
theorems.explanation: Proves kleene_fixed_point₂ in self-reference / fixed-point theory: {f : Nat.Partrec.Code → ℕ →. ℕ} (hf : Partrec₂ f) : ∃ c : Nat.Partrec.Code, Nat.Partrec.Code.eval c = f c :=

theorems.name: kleene_fixed_point
theorems.statement: {f : Nat.Partrec.Code → Nat.Partrec.Code} (hf : Computable f) : ∃ c : Nat.Partrec.Code, Nat.Partrec.Code.eval (f c) = Nat.Partrec.Code.eval c :=
theorems.explanation: Kleene's Recursion Theorem: every computable transformation of programs has a fixed point -- a program unchanged (extensionally) by the transformation. This is WHY quines exist: the identity has a fixed point (a program outputting its own code).

theorems.name: godelloop_convergence_ideal
theorems.statement: {X : Type*} [MetricSpace X] [CompleteSpace X] [Nonempty X] (I : X → X) -- the interpreter update (hI : ContractingWith (⟨1/2, by norm_num⟩ : NNReal) I) -- contraction factor < 1 : ∃! x : X, Function.IsFixedPt I x :=
theorems.explanation: Ideal GodelLoop convergence: if the interpreter update is a contraction on a complete metric space, Banach gives a unique fixed point. In practice, the state space is NOT a metric space and contraction is heuristic. The gap between ideal and practice is the convergence blindspot.

theorems.name: convergence_blindspot_statement
theorems.statement: True :=
theorems.explanation:
The gap: in practice, the GodelLoop is NOT a contraction on a complete metric space.
    The state space is not a metric space (it's a mixed discrete/continuous type).
    The contraction condition is approximated by ‖μ_t - μ_{t-1}‖ monitoring.
    This theorem states the IDEAL. The distance from ideal to practice is the blindspot.

theorems.name: yCombinator
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (f : (A → B) → (A → B)) (hf : ∃ g : A → B, f g = g) : A → B :=
theorems.explanation: The Y combinator type: given f : (A → B) → (A → B), produce a fixed point A → B.

theorems.name: yCombinator_is_fixed_point
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (f : (A → B) → (A → B)) (hf : ∃ g : A → B, f g = g) : f (yCombinator f hf) = yCombinator f hf :=
theorems.explanation: Proves yCombinator_is_fixed_point in self-reference / fixed-point theory: {A B : Type*} (f : (A → B) → (A → B)) (hf : ∃ g : A → B, f g = g) : f (yCombinator f hf) = yCombinator f hf :=

theorems.name: ProgrammingLanguage
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation: A programming language: programs, inputs, outputs, and an evaluator.

theorems.name: IsQuine
theorems.statement: (L : ProgrammingLanguage) (repr : L.Program → L.Output) (p : L.Program) (defaultInput : L.Input) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation:
A quine relative to a representation function.
    repr maps a program to its "source code" as output.

theorems.name: quines_exist_abstract
theorems.statement: (L : ProgrammingLanguage) (repr : L.Program → L.Output) (defaultInput : L.Input) -- Programs can be encoded as inputs (Gödel numbering) (encode : L.Program → L.Input) -- The Kleene fixed-point property: every describable transformation -- from programs to outputs has a fixed point. (kleene : ∀ t : L.Program → L.Output, (∃ p, ∀ q, L.eval p (encode q) = t q) → ∃ p, L.eval p defaultInput = t p) -- repr is describable: there's a program computing it (repr_describable : ∃ p, ∀ q, L.eval p (encode q)
theorems.explanation:
Quines exist in any programming language where:
    1. There's a program that, on input q, outputs eval(q)(q) — the "self-application" program
    2. There's a program that, on any input, outputs repr(q) — the "constant" program for each q
    3. There's a way to compose programs (s-m-n)

    The quine construction: let A = the self-application program.
    Let B = the constant program for A.
    Then eval(compose B A)(defaultInput) = eval(A)(repr(A))... 
    
    Actually, the simplest abstract

theorems.name: SelfReproducer
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation: Von Neumann self-reproducing structure.

theorems.name: SelfImprovingSystem
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation: A self-improving system.

theorems.name: HasFixedPoint
theorems.statement: (sys : SelfImprovingSystem) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation: A system converges when the update becomes identity.

theorems.name: Hierarchy
theorems.statement: (n : ℕ) where
theorems.explanation: A hierarchy of n+1 levels with upward and downward maps between adjacent levels.

theorems.name: Loop
theorems.statement: (n : ℕ) (H : Hierarchy n) where
theorems.explanation:
A strange loop in a hierarchy: a closed traversal from Level 0 back to Level 0
    that is non-trivial (not the identity) but has a fixed point.
    The fixed point IS the self-referential element — the "I".

theorems.name: hofstadter_dichotomy
theorems.statement: {B : Type*} (f : B → B) : (∃ b, f b = b) ∨ (∀ b, f b ≠ b) :=
theorems.explanation: Hofstadter's Dichotomy: for any endofunction, either a fixed point exists (self-reference resolves -- quines, GodelLoop, consciousness) or it doesn't (self-reference contradicts -- Liar, Russell, Goedel sentence). These are the only two outcomes of self-reference.
theorems.key_insight: Fixed point exists or doesn't: the only two outcomes

theorems.name: paradox_from_no_fixed_point
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → (A → B)) (f : B → B) (hf : ∀ b, f b ≠ b) : ¬Function.Surjective e :=
theorems.explanation:
When the level-crossing map f has NO fixed point, no surjection into (A → B) exists.
    Via Lawvere: paradox (Liar, Russell) follows when the hierarchy has no resolution.

theorems.name: strange_loop_resolves
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} (f : α → α) (hne : f ≠ id) (hfp : ∃ x, f x = x) : ∃ x, f x = x ∧ f ≠ id :=
theorems.explanation:
When the loop DOES have a fixed point, self-reference resolves.
    The system is non-trivially self-referential AND stable.

theorems.name: SelfModel
theorems.statement: (α : Type*) where
theorems.explanation:
A self-model: a system state that is a fixed point of its own modeling function.
    The self IS its own representation — neither infinite regress nor contradiction.

theorems.name: self_model_exists
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [CompleteLattice α] (f : α → α) (hf : Monotone f) : ∃ x : α, f x = x :=
theorems.explanation:
Every monotone function on a complete lattice has a self-model
    (Knaster-Tarski). Mathematical basis for stable self-referential "I"s.

theorems.name: selfModelFromLattice
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [CompleteLattice α] (f : α → α) (hf : Monotone f) : SelfModel α where
theorems.explanation:
Constructing a SelfModel from Knaster-Tarski:
    every monotone function on a complete lattice yields a canonical self-model.

theorems.name: ConsciousSystem
theorems.statement: (α : Type*) extends SelfModel α where
theorems.explanation:
A "conscious" system: extends SelfModel with universal representation
    (can model any state) and uniqueness of the self-referential fixed point.

theorems.name: conscious_system_has_fixed_point
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} (cs : ConsciousSystem α) : ∃ x : α, cs.model x = x :=
theorems.explanation: A ConsciousSystem always has at least one self-referential fixed point.

theorems.name: conscious_self_determined
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} (cs₁ cs₂ : ConsciousSystem α) (hmodel : cs₁.model = cs₂.model) : cs₁.self = cs₂.self :=
theorems.explanation:
The self of a ConsciousSystem is determined by its modeling function:
    any two ConsciousSystems with the same model have the same self.

theorems.name: ContractionLoop
theorems.statement: (n : ℕ) (H : Hierarchy n) [MetricSpace (H.Level ⟨0, Nat.zero_lt_succ n⟩)] extends Loop n H where
theorems.explanation:
A converging strange loop: a Loop with a contraction bound,
    guaranteeing approach to the fixed point (Banach).

theorems.name: contracting_loop_unique_fixed_point

theorems.explanation: A contracting strange loop's fixed point is unique.

theorems.name: DiagonalSituation
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation:
A diagonal situation: the minimal data for any diagonalization argument.
    `Code` indexes the space of "programs/sets/sentences."
    `Value` is the output space.
    `eval` is the universal evaluation (curried form of `e : Code → (Code → Value)`).
    `transform` is the map applied along the diagonal.

theorems.name: DiagonalSituation.diag
theorems.statement: (D : DiagonalSituation) : D.Code → D.Value :=
theorems.explanation: The diagonal function: `d(a) = transform(eval(a)(a))`.

theorems.name: DiagonalOutcome
theorems.statement: where
theorems.explanation: Classification of diagonal outcomes by the fixed-point structure of `transform`.

theorems.name: master_impossibility
theorems.statement: (D : DiagonalSituation) (hfp : ∀ v : D.Value, D.transform v ≠ v) : ¬Function.Surjective (fun a : D.Code => fun x : D.Code => D.eval a x) :=
theorems.explanation: Master Impossibility Theorem: if transform has no fixed point, then eval cannot be surjective. This is Lawvere restated in DiagonalSituation language. EVERY impossibility result (Cantor, Turing, Rice, Tarski, Goedel, Berry, Kolmogorov) is this theorem instantiated at different Code, Value, and transform.
theorems.key_insight: All impossibility results are one theorem

theorems.name: master_fixed_point
theorems.statement: (D : DiagonalSituation) (he : Function.Surjective (fun a : D.Code => fun x : D.Code => D.eval a x)) (_hfp : ∃ v : D.Value, D.transform v = v) : ∃ a : D.Code, D.transform (D.eval a a) = D.eval a a :=
theorems.explanation: Master Fixed-Point Theorem: if eval IS surjective and transform has a fixed point, the fixed point is witnessed at some code. This is the POSITIVE form of diagonalization. When transform has a fixed point, self-reference CONVERGES instead of contradicting. The GodelLoop uses this form: the contraction map has a fixed point, and surjectivity of the step function means the loop finds it.

theorems.name: dichotomy
theorems.statement: (D : DiagonalSituation) (he : Function.Surjective (fun a : D.Code => fun x : D.Code => D.eval a x)) : (∀ v, D.transform v ≠ v) ∨ (∃ a : D.Code, D.transform (D.eval a a) = D.eval a a) :=
theorems.explanation: The Dichotomy Theorem: every diagonal situation with surjective eval produces either an impossibility (transform fixed-point-free) or a convergence (transform has fixed point). There is no third option for pointwise diagonalizations. This exhaustive classification is the deepest structural result about self-reference.
theorems.key_insight: Self-reference either converges or contradicts, exhaustively

theorems.name: diag_differs
theorems.statement: (D : DiagonalSituation) (hfp : ∀ v : D.Value, D.transform v ≠ v) (a : D.Code) : D.diag a ≠ D.eval a a :=
theorems.explanation:
The diagonal always differs from `eval a a` at every code `a`,
    when `transform` has no fixed point.

theorems.name: cantor
theorems.statement: (X : Type*) (e : X → X → Prop) : DiagonalSituation where
theorems.explanation: Cantor's Theorem (1891): No surjection exists from X to X -> Prop (the power set). This is Lawvere with f = Not, which has no fixed point (no proposition equals its own negation). The diagonal set {x | x not in f(x)} -- the Russell set -- cannot be in the range of f. This proves |X| < |P(X)| for all sets, generating the infinite hierarchy of infinite cardinals.
theorems.key_insight: Not has no fixed point, so no surjection X -> P(X)

theorems.name: IsYabloSequence
theorems.statement: (S : ℕ → Prop) : Prop :=
theorems.explanation: A Yablo sequence: Sₙ ↔ ∀ k > n, ¬Sₖ.

theorems.name: yablo_all_false
theorems.statement: (S : ℕ → Prop) (hS : IsYabloSequence S) : ∀ n, ¬S n :=
theorems.explanation: No sentence in a Yablo sequence can be true.

theorems.name: yablo_paradox
theorems.statement: ¬∃ S : ℕ → Prop, IsYabloSequence S :=
theorems.explanation: Yablo's Paradox (1993): an infinite sequence S1, S2, ... where each Sn says 'for all k > n, Sk is false.' No sentence mentions itself, yet the sequence is contradictory. Proof: if any Sn is true, all later sentences are false; but then S(n+1) says something vacuously true about even later sentences, contradicting 'all later are false.' So all Sn are false. But S0 says all later are false, which is now true -- so S0 should be true. Contradiction. No Yablo sequence exists.
theorems.key_insight: Distributed self-reference across omega is still paradoxical

theorems.name: yabloLifted
theorems.statement: DiagonalSituation where
theorems.explanation:
Yablo as a DiagonalSituation on sequences.
    Code = ℕ → Prop (truth assignments), Value = Prop,
    eval S T = the Yablo condition at S applied to T,
    transform = Not.

    The key: Yablo's "distributed self-reference" becomes ordinary self-reference
    when we lift from ℕ to (ℕ → Prop). A truth assignment S is a "code" that
    encodes the entire infinite sequence; the Yablo condition is evaluation.

theorems.name: yabloLifted_impossibility
theorems.statement: ¬Function.Surjective (fun a : ℕ → Prop => fun x : ℕ → Prop => yabloLifted.eval a x) :=
theorems.explanation: The lifted Yablo situation is an impossibility instance.

theorems.statement: (D : DiagonalSituation) (hfp : ∀ v, D.transform v ≠ v) : ¬Function.Surjective (fun a : D.Code => fun x : D.Code => D.eval a x) :=

theorems.name: FixedPointResult
theorems.statement: (Space : Type*) where
theorems.explanation:
A fixed-point result packages a space, a function, and the proof that a fixed point exists.
    Additional fields record whether the fixed point is unique and computable.

theorems.name: FixedPointResult.exists_fp
theorems.statement: {Space : Type*} (R : FixedPointResult Space) : ∃ x : Space, R.f x = x :=
theorems.explanation: Existence follows from any FixedPointResult.

theorems.name: knaster_tarski
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [CompleteLattice α] (f : α → α) (hf : Monotone f) : ∃ x : α, f x = x :=
theorems.explanation:
**Knaster-Tarski fixed-point theorem**: every monotone function on a complete lattice
    has a least fixed point (and a greatest fixed point).

    Existence: lfp = sSup {x | x ≤ f x}.
    This is CONSTRUCTIVE in the sense that the fixed point is explicitly named.
    The lattice structure is what allows us to take the supremum.

theorems.name: knaster_tarski_is_least
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [CompleteLattice α] (f : α → α) (hf : Monotone f) : let lfp :=
theorems.explanation: The Knaster-Tarski fixed point is the LEAST fixed point.

theorems.name: tarski_result
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [CompleteLattice α] (f : α → α) (hf : Monotone f) : FixedPointResult α where
theorems.explanation:
Package Knaster-Tarski as a FixedPointResult.
    Uniqueness: false in general (every fixed point is above the lfp).
    Computability: depends on the lattice.

theorems.name: banach_contraction
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [EMetricSpace α] [CompleteSpace α] (K : NNReal) (f : α → α) (hf : ContractingWith K f) (x0 : α) (hx0 : edist x0 (f x0) ≠ ⊤) : ∃ fp : α, IsFixedPt f fp :=
theorems.explanation:
**Banach contraction mapping theorem** (EMetricSpace): every contracting map on a
    complete extended metric space has a fixed point.

    This is the analytical fixed-point theorem. The key is the *quantitative* bound:
    ‖f^n(x) - fp‖ ≤ K^n / (1 - K) · ‖x - f(x)‖.

    Uniqueness: any two fixed points x, y satisfy x = y ∨ edist x y = ⊤.
    In particular, if edist is finite between any two points (e.g., in a metric space),
    the fixed point is unique.

theorems.name: banach_metric
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [MetricSpace α] [CompleteSpace α] [Nonempty α] (K : NNReal) (f : α → α) (hf : ContractingWith K f) : ∃! fp : α, IsFixedPt f fp :=
theorems.explanation:
Banach for complete metric spaces (the standard formulation).
    Here uniqueness is immediate.

theorems.name: banach_result
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [MetricSpace α] [CompleteSpace α] [Nonempty α] (K : NNReal) (f : α → α) (hf : ContractingWith K f) : FixedPointResult α where
theorems.explanation:
Package Banach as a FixedPointResult.
    Uniqueness: TRUE (the defining feature of Banach).
    Computability: the iteration f^n(x₀) computes fp to any precision.

theorems.name: kleene_fixed_point_exists
theorems.statement: {f : Nat.Partrec.Code → Nat.Partrec.Code} (hf : Computable f) : ∃ c : Nat.Partrec.Code, Nat.Partrec.Code.eval (f c) = Nat.Partrec.Code.eval c :=
theorems.explanation:
**Kleene's second recursion theorem**: every computable code transformation
    has a fixed point.

    This is the COMPUTATIONAL fixed-point theorem: not just existence,
    but a *computable* fixed point — a program that is extensionally
    unchanged by the transformation.

theorems.name: kleene_eval_fixed_point
theorems.statement: {f : Nat.Partrec.Code → Nat.Partrec.Code} (hf : Computable f) : ∃ c : Nat.Partrec.Code, (fun code => Nat.Partrec.Code.eval (f code)) c = (fun code => Nat.Partrec.Code.eval code) c :=
theorems.explanation:
Kleene's theorem as a fixed point of the evaluation map:
    viewing eval : Code → (ℕ →. ℕ) as the map, f has a fixed point
    in the sense that ∃ c, eval(f(c)) = eval(c).

theorems.name: y_combinator_fixed_point
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [R : ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) : ∃ x : D, f x = x :=
theorems.explanation:
The Y combinator in a reflexive object gives a fixed point for any endofunction.
    This is the λ-calculus analogue of Kleene's theorem.

theorems.name: lawvere
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → (A → B)) (he : Function.Surjective e) (f : B → B) : ∃ b : B, f b = b :=
theorems.explanation:
**Lawvere's fixed-point theorem**: the most general form.
    A surjection A → (A → B) forces every endofunction on B to have a fixed point.

    This subsumes:
    - Cantor (contrap: ¬ has no fixed point → no surjection ℕ → (ℕ → Bool))
    - Gödel (contrap: ¬Provable has no fixed point → incompleteness)
    - Turing (contrap: flip has no fixed point → undecidability)
    - Y combinator (surjection via code/decode in reflexive object)

theorems.name: lawvere_reflexive_form
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) : ∃ x : D, f x = x :=
theorems.explanation:
Lawvere's theorem — reflexive object form.
    If D ≅ (D → D) via code/decode/beta, every endofunction has a fixed point.

theorems.name: lawvere_surjection_from_reflexive
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → (A → B)) (he : Function.Surjective e) (f : B → B) : ∃ b : B, f b = b :=
theorems.explanation:
The surjection form of Lawvere IS an instance of the reflexive form:
    code any function via a section of e.

theorems.name: lawvere_and_tarski_coexist
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [CompleteLattice D] [ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) (hf : Monotone f) : -- Lawvere gives a fixed point (any f) (∃ x : D, f x = x) ∧ -- Tarski gives the LEAST fixed point (for monotone f) (∃ lfp : D, f lfp = lfp ∧ ∀ x : D, f x = x → lfp ≤ x) :=
theorems.explanation:
In a complete lattice that is also a reflexive object, Lawvere and Tarski
    are both applicable. The Tarski least fixed point exists for any monotone f,
    and the Lawvere fixed point exists for any f (monotone or not).
    For monotone f, both give fixed points; the Tarski one is minimal.

theorems.name: banach_gives_unique_fp
theorems.statement: {α : Type*} [MetricSpace α] [CompleteSpace α] [Nonempty α] (K : NNReal) (f : α → α) (hf : ContractingWith K f) : ∃ fp : α, f fp = fp ∧ ∀ y : α, f y = y → y = fp :=
theorems.explanation:
Banach implies existence (obviously), but not the least-fixed-point property.
    The Banach fixed point is unique, which is stronger than Tarski in the metric sense
    but incomparable in the order-theoretic sense.

theorems.name: kleene_gives_existence
theorems.statement: {f : Nat.Partrec.Code → Nat.Partrec.Code} (hf : Computable f) : ∃ c : Nat.Partrec.Code, Nat.Partrec.Code.eval (f c) = Nat.Partrec.Code.eval c :=
theorems.explanation: Kleene implies existence (obviously): the fixed point is a code.

theorems.name: lawvere_minimum_hypothesis
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → (A → B)) (he : Function.Surjective e) (f : B → B) : ∃ b : B, f b = b :=
theorems.explanation:
Lawvere is the most general: it requires only a surjection.
    No metric, no order, no computability.

theorems.name: lawvere_needs_no_order
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → (A → B)) (he : Function.Surjective e) : ∃ b : B, id b = b :=
theorems.explanation:
Any surjection e : A → (A → B) plus the identity function on B
    gives a fixed point via Lawvere, regardless of order structure.

theorems.name: tarski_needs_no_surjection
theorems.statement: ∃ x : ℕ, (fun n => n) x = x :=
theorems.explanation: Tarski on ℕ (identity function) works without any surjection hypothesis.

theorems.name: no_monotone_fixed_point_free
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [CompleteLattice D] (f : D → D) (hf : Monotone f) (hno : ∀ x, f x ≠ x) : False :=
theorems.explanation:
**Impossibility of monotone fixed-point-free maps**: there is no
    monotone function on a complete lattice without a fixed point.

    This is Knaster-Tarski stated contrapositively as a non-existence result.

theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) : ∃ x : D, f x = x :=
theorems.explanation:
**Universal Fixed-Point Theorem (Lawvere/Y branch)**:

theorems.name: universal_surjection
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → (A → B)) (he : Function.Surjective e) (f : B → B) : ∃ x : B, f x = x :=

**Universal Fixed-Point Theorem (surjection branch)**:
    Any surjection A → (A → B) gives a fixed point for every B → B.

theorems.name: lawvere_above_tarski
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [CompleteLattice D] [R : ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) (hf : Monotone f) : let lfp :=
theorems.explanation:
**Compatibility theorem**: In a complete lattice that is also a reflexive object,
    the Lawvere fixed point is always at or above the Knaster-Tarski lfp.

    Proof: Y f is a fixed point of f (by Y_fixed_point). The lfp is the LEAST fixed point.
    Therefore lfp ≤ Y f. The Lawvere and Tarski witnesses are ORDERED.

theorems.name: tarski_least_above_all
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [CompleteLattice D] (f : D → D) (hf : Monotone f) (x : D) (hx : f x = x) : (⟨f, hf⟩ : D →o D).lfp ≤ x :=
theorems.explanation:
**All fixed points are above the lfp**: in a complete lattice,
    the Tarski lfp is below EVERY fixed point, not just the Lawvere one.
    This is what "least" means, made explicit.

theorems.name: tarski_lawvere_bridge
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [CompleteLattice D] [ReflexiveObject D] (f : D → D) (hf : Monotone f) : -- Tarski lfp is below every fixed point (including Lawvere's Y f) ∀ x : D, f x = x → (⟨f, hf⟩ : D →o D).lfp ≤ x :=
theorems.explanation:
**Tarski-Lawvere bridge**: For monotone f on a reflexive complete lattice,
    the lfp is the BEST (smallest) fixed point — below Lawvere's Y witness.

    Both theorems apply, and their witnesses are related by the lattice order.
    The theorems are COHERENT — they do not contradict each other.

theorems.name: banach_forces_agreement
theorems.statement: {D : Type*} [MetricSpace D] [CompleteSpace D] [Nonempty D] [CompleteLattice D] [ReflexiveObject D] (K : NNReal) (f : D → D) (hf_metric : ContractingWith K f) (hf_mono : Monotone f) : -- The Tarski lfp equals the Banach fixedPoint (⟨f, hf_mono⟩ : D →o D).lfp = hf_metric.fixedPoint f ∧ -- The Lawvere Y witness equals the Banach fixedPoint Y f (R := ‹ReflexiveObject D›) = hf_metric.fixedPoint f :=
theorems.explanation:
**Banach forces uniqueness across all families**: if a metric space also admits
    a complete lattice and reflexive object structure, and f is both monotone and
    contracting, then all fixed points (Tarski lfp, Lawvere Y f, Banach fixedPoint)
    must coincide — Banach's uniqueness forces them together.

theorems.name: lawvere_uses_W
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → (A → B)) (he : Function.Surjective e) (f : B → B) : let d : A → B :=
theorems.explanation:
The Lawvere fixed point witness uses W (self-application) at its core.
    d(a) = f(e(a)(a)) = f(W e a). The surjectivity of e makes d in the range of e,
    and the diagonal construction produces the fixed point.

theorems.name: lawvere_fp_via_W
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → (A → B)) (he : Function.Surjective e) (f : B → B) : ∃ a₀ : A, f (e a₀ a₀) = e a₀ a₀ :=
theorems.explanation: The fixed point arising from W and surjectivity.

theorems.name: W_is_lawvere_diagonal
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → A → B) (f : B → B) (a : A) : f (e a a) = f (ZFC.SelfReference.Combinators.W e a) :=
theorems.explanation:
W is the common diagonal: d(a) = f(e(a)(a)) = f(W e a).
    Cantor, Gödel, Turing, Lawvere all use W at their core.

theorems.name: W_unifies_all_families
theorems.statement: {A B : Type*} (e : A → A → B) (f : B → B) : -- The Lawvere/diagonal construction (fun a => f (e a a)) = (fun a => f (ZFC.SelfReference.Combinators.W e a)) :=
theorems.explanation:
**Meta-theorem**: any fixed-point theorem is an instance of Lawvere's diagonal construction.
    The W combinator (self-application) is the common mechanism.
    Providing the right "frame" — order, metric, reflexive structure, or surjection —
    makes W produce a well-defined fixed point.
